title: The Unwritten Rules of Japanese Trains 窶・Read This Before You Board
excerpt: Japan窶冱 trains run on time to the minute and operate on a set of social rules that nobody posts on a sign. Here窶冱 what they are.
heroImageAlt: The interior of a Tokyo commuter train with passengers sitting quietly
author: TABI Editorial

sections.heading: Silent mode means silent
paragraphs: The first thing Japanese train operators ask is that you set your phone to manner mode (silent). This means no ringtone, no notification sounds, no speaker audio of any kind. It does not mean vibrate. In a quiet carriage, a buzzing phone on a hard surface is clearly audible to the six people nearest you. Put it fully silent. This takes two seconds and prevents one of the more uncomfortable social experiences available to a visitor.

sections.heading: Phone calls are not done
paragraphs: Taking a phone call on a Japanese commuter train is a social violation roughly equivalent to eating a sandwich in a library. The standard approach is to let it go to voicemail, or to answer, say quietly that you are on a train and will call back, and end the call immediately. This is what Japanese commuters do. On long-distance Shinkansen, calls are generally acceptable in the space between carriages. In the carriage itself, the same rules apply.

sections.heading: Priority seats
paragraphs: The seats marked with a priority label near the carriage doors (often in a different color or fabric) are reserved for elderly passengers, pregnant women, passengers with young children, and those with injuries or disabilities. On a crowded train, these seats fill up like any other. On a less crowded train, the norm is to leave them empty unless you are one of the intended users. The nuance: at 11pm on a Friday, nobody enforces this strictly. At 8am on a Monday, a healthy young person sitting in a priority seat while an elderly passenger stands nearby is making a choice that other passengers will notice.

sections.heading: Platform queuing
paragraphs: Most Tokyo platforms have painted lines on the ground showing where train doors will open and which direction passengers should queue. You queue behind the lines, in two parallel columns, and you let people off the train before you board. This is not complicated, but it is enforced by social pressure rather than barriers, and it fails immediately when visitors don窶冲 understand the system. Look at the floor. Find the lines. Stand in them.

sections.heading: Bags and backpacks
paragraphs: On a crowded train, a backpack worn on your back occupies the space of an additional person and hits other passengers every time you turn. The protocol is to take it off and either hold it in front of you or place it in the overhead luggage shelf. Large rolling suitcases go in the space at the end of the carriage near the doors, not in the aisle. This is an issue specifically at peak hours on the Narita Express and on Shinkansen platforms during tourist seasons.

sections.heading: Eating on trains
paragraphs: Eating on local commuter trains is generally not done. Drinking from a closed container is fine. On long-distance Shinkansen, eating is acceptable and expected 窶・the ekiben (station bento) culture exists specifically for this. The distinction is between a 10-minute metro ride and a two-hour bullet train journey. If in doubt, the rule is: if the person next to you isn窶冲 eating, you probably shouldn窶冲 be either.

sections.heading: Why these rules matter
paragraphs: Japan窶冱 train system carries millions of people every day with near-zero conflict. The reason is not enforcement 窶・there are no transit police watching for phone calls or checking priority seats. The reason is that everybody in the carriage has implicitly agreed to the same set of rules, and the rules work because they are followed consistently. A visitor who doesn窶冲 know them isn窶冲 being judged harshly; they窶决e just outside the system. Knowing them before you board means you spend the journey looking at the city, not at an awkward situation.

title: How to Visit a Japanese Temple or Shrine Without Being Accidentally Rude
excerpt: Temples and shrines have different rules, different purposes, and different gods. Getting them confused is the starting mistake. Everything else follows from there.
heroImageAlt: A stone torii gate leading to a Shinto shrine path lined with lanterns
author: TABI Editorial

sections.heading: Temple vs shrine 窶・the difference matters
paragraphs: Temples (o-tera) are Buddhist. Shrines (jinja) are Shinto. The buildings look different, the rituals are different, and the correct behavior is slightly different at each. The fastest way to tell them apart: if there is a torii gate 窶・the distinctive arch, usually red or stone 窶・you are at a shrine. If there is a large incense burner and a main hall with Buddha imagery, you are at a temple. Many famous sites have both on the same grounds, particularly in Kyoto, because Buddhism and Shinto coexisted for centuries.

sections.heading: The torii gate
paragraphs: Walking through a torii gate marks the transition from the ordinary world into sacred space. Walk through it, not around it. The center of the path through a shrine is considered the route of the deity; some people walk slightly to one side as a gesture of deference. This is not required of visitors, but it窶冱 the kind of thing worth knowing exists.

sections.heading: Temizuya: the purification fountain
paragraphs: Near the entrance of both shrines and many temples there is a temizuya 窶・a stone basin with ladles. The ritual: take a ladle with your right hand, pour water over your left hand. Transfer the ladle to your left hand, pour over your right. Pour a small amount into your left palm and rinse your mouth (spit to the side, not into the basin). Pour the remaining water from the ladle over both hands simultaneously, tilting the ladle so the water runs down the handle rather than being poured from the bowl. Most foreign visitors skip this entirely and it窶冱 not obligatory, but doing it correctly is a straightforward gesture of respect.

sections.heading: How to pray at a shrine
paragraphs: Approach the main hall (haiden). Toss a coin into the offering box 窶・five yen (go-en) coins are traditional because 窶枠o-en窶・also means 窶枠ood fortune.窶・Ring the bell if there is one. Bow deeply twice. Clap twice. Pray or hold the moment. Bow once more. That窶冱 the full sequence. You don窶冲 need to remember all of it. Two deep bows and two claps gets you 80% of the way there and nobody will correct you.

sections.heading: How to pray at a temple
paragraphs: Buddhist temples don窶冲 involve clapping. The general approach: place a coin in the offering box, place your hands together (gassho), bow, and hold the moment. Incense burners at temples allow you to light a stick and wave the smoke toward yourself 窶・considered purifying, and particularly common for waving smoke toward aching joints. Buy the incense at the counter, not from someone else窶冱 bundle.

sections.heading: Photography
paragraphs: Photography of the exterior grounds is almost always permitted. Photography inside the main hall is often not, and there will usually be a sign. If there is no sign and you are unsure, look at what other visitors are doing. Taking a photograph while someone else is praying is poor form regardless of whether signs permit it. At some Kyoto shrines, photography at certain hours has been restricted specifically because of past overcrowding by tourists 窶・check in advance for Fushimi Inari and Gion Shrine.

sections.heading: What to buy
paragraphs: Omamori are amulets sold at most shrines and temples, intended for specific purposes: safe travel, good health, exam success. They are meant to be replaced annually 窶・return the old one to the shrine when you buy a new one, or dispose of it respectfully. Ema are wooden plaques for writing wishes and prayers, hung on a rack at the shrine. You don窶冲 need to write in Japanese; English works. The wish is between you and whatever you are choosing to address.

title: Why You Should Never Tip in Japan 窶・and What to Do Instead
excerpt: In Japan, the service is built into the price. Adding a tip doesn窶冲 reward the server 窶・it creates an awkward situation for both of you.
heroImageAlt: A server at a traditional Japanese restaurant carefully placing dishes on a low table
author: TABI Editorial

sections.heading: The rule
paragraphs: Do not tip in Japan. Not at restaurants. Not in taxis. Not at the ryokan. Not at the hotel. The rule applies everywhere, with essentially no exceptions. This is not a matter of the tip being too small or the service not meriting it. Tipping is simply not part of the service culture, and attempting it creates confusion rather than goodwill.

sections.heading: Why tipping is not done
paragraphs: In Japan, service is understood as part of what you paid for. The waiter who refills your water, the taxi driver who opens and closes the door automatically from the front seat, the ryokan attendant who arranges your futon 窶・they are doing their job with the full understanding that it will be done to a high standard because that is the expectation, not because there is a financial incentive to exceed it. Tipping implies that the service was beyond what was expected, which suggests the expectation was low. This is not the message most people intend to send.

sections.heading: What happens if you try
paragraphs: If you leave cash on the table at a restaurant, staff will often chase you down the street to return it, assuming you forgot it. If you hand money directly to a server as a tip, it is likely to be declined, or accepted with visible discomfort. In rare cases it may be accepted with genuine bewilderment. None of these outcomes is the one you were hoping for.

sections.heading: At a ryokan
paragraphs: Ryokan are the one context where something resembling a tip exists 窶・but it operates on different logic. An o-shibori (a small envelope with a gratuity inside) can be given to the ryokan attendant assigned to your room on arrival, as a gesture of appreciation for the personal service throughout the stay. This is not expected and is not common among foreign guests. If you want to do it, the amount is typically ﾂ･1,000窶・,000, placed in an envelope (not loose cash, never loose cash). The envelope matters as much as the money.

sections.heading: In taxis
paragraphs: Taxi fare is metered. You pay the meter. You do not round up. You do not add anything. The driver will make precise change for whatever you give them, and expect you to take it. Declining the change and saying 窶鰐eep it窶・will land as strange, not generous.

sections.heading: What to do instead
paragraphs: What Japanese culture does value in place of tips: acknowledgment. A genuine 窶和rigatou gozaimashita窶・(thank you very much) at the end of a meal, a bow at the door of the ryokan, a simple gesture of appreciation that the person can receive without awkwardness. The service culture in Japan is built on mutual respect that doesn窶冲 require cash to function. The best thing you can do is participate in it on its own terms.

title: When to Take Your Shoes Off in Japan: The Complete Guide
excerpt: The shoe rule in Japan is not arbitrary. It reflects a clear line between outside space and inside space that structures daily life. Here is where that line is drawn.
heroImageAlt: A row of shoes neatly arranged in a traditional Japanese entryway genkan
author: TABI Editorial

sections.heading: The genkan
paragraphs: Traditional Japanese homes, ryokan, and many older buildings have a genkan 窶・an entrance area slightly lower than the rest of the floor, designed specifically for removing shoes. You step into the genkan, take off your shoes, and step up into the interior in socks or bare feet. The directional convention: shoes are placed pointing toward the door, so they窶决e ready to put on when you leave. This isn窶冲 required of guests, but it窶冱 what you窶冤l observe.

sections.heading: At ryokan
paragraphs: At a ryokan, shoes come off at the main entrance and you窶决e given slippers. These slippers go everywhere inside the building except tatami rooms (where you go in socks or bare feet) and the toilet (which has its own dedicated pair). The slippers-off-at-tatami rule catches most visitors at least once. Look at the floor: if it窶冱 tatami, your slippers stay outside.

sections.heading: At some restaurants
paragraphs: Traditional Japanese restaurants 窶・particularly those with tatami seating or private rooms 窶・will have a designated shoe removal area near the entrance. The signal is usually a raised floor, a row of slippers, or a staff member making the gesture. If you窶决e unsure, look at what other guests near the entrance are doing. Western-style restaurants in Japan do not require shoe removal.

sections.heading: At some temples
paragraphs: Certain temple halls require shoes to be removed before entering. There will be a step at the entrance and either a rack for shoes or plastic bags to carry them inside with you. This varies by temple. Fushimi Inari in Kyoto does not require shoe removal for the main torii paths. Todai-ji in Nara does for the main hall interior. The entrance step is the consistent signal.

sections.heading: The toilet slipper trap
paragraphs: The most reliable mistake visitors make: walking out of the toilet in the toilet slippers. At ryokan and some traditional homes, there is a dedicated pair of slippers inside the toilet room. You put them on to enter, and you take them off when you leave. Walking back through the inn in toilet slippers is the equivalent of walking through the house carrying the toilet brush. It happens to almost everyone once. The correction is to look down at your feet before exiting the toilet.

sections.heading: Reading the signals
paragraphs: The consistent signal that shoes come off: a step at the entrance that marks a change in floor level. When you see a step, assume shoes off until you have evidence otherwise. Modern hotels, convenience stores, restaurants with Western seating, and most commercial buildings do not follow this rule. The genkan-step is the dividing line.

title: How to Behave in Public in Japan: The Unwritten Rules
excerpt: Japanese public spaces operate on a set of unspoken rules that make them exceptionally pleasant. Most visitors don窶冲 know them. All of them are learnable in ten minutes.
heroImageAlt: A busy pedestrian crossing in Tokyo with people moving in orderly streams
author: TABI Editorial

sections.heading: Don窶冲 eat while walking
paragraphs: Eating while walking is generally not done in Japan. The exception is festival food from stalls at a matsuri (festival), where the eating-while-standing context is explicit. The rule applies elsewhere: you finish your konbini onigiri standing outside the shop, not while walking down the street. Street food vendors at tourist sites have started providing small eating areas specifically because this norm exists and tourists don窶冲 know it. Use them.

sections.heading: Queuing is not optional
paragraphs: Japan queues. It queues at ATMs, at crosswalks, at train doors, at ramen shops, at theme park rides. Cutting in line is an extremely unwelcome behavior. If there is no visible queue but a crowd, look for the end of it and join there. Lines at Japanese institutions are remarkably orderly and self-policing 窶・no one will say anything if you push in, but everyone will notice, and the social discomfort this creates for you is real and immediate.

sections.heading: Noise levels
paragraphs: Japan is quieter than most countries in public spaces. Not silent 窶・Shinjuku at 6pm is genuinely loud 窶・but the background noise level in trains, restaurants, and streets comes from the environment rather than individual conversations. Speaking at a volume that carries beyond your table in a restaurant, laughing loudly in a way that commands the room窶冱 attention, or holding a speakerphone conversation in a convenience store are all behaviors that will cause people to move away from you. This is the social feedback system.

sections.heading: Escalators
paragraphs: On escalators, the standing side is the left. The right side is for walking. This is the Tokyo convention; Osaka does it in reverse (stand right, walk left) and this causes visible confusion at every point where Tokyoites and Osakans intersect. When in doubt, follow the person in front of you. The escalator rule is enforced entirely by social pressure and the occasional polite request to stand to one side, but it is real and consistent.

sections.heading: There are no rubbish bins
paragraphs: Japan removed most public rubbish bins following a 1995 sarin gas attack. This means that in most public spaces 窶・streets, parks, station platforms 窶・there is nowhere to throw away your waste. You carry it with you until you reach a convenience store (which has a bin near the register), a restaurant, or your accommodation. Konbini is the practical answer for most of the day. This is not as inconvenient as it sounds once you accept that the rubbish is coming with you.

sections.heading: Photography of strangers
paragraphs: Photographing people without consent in Japan operates under the same social norms as most countries, but with one additional consideration: the geisha districts of Kyoto (particularly Gion) have become sites of harassment as visitors photograph geiko and maiko at close range. Some streets in Gion now restrict photography entirely during certain hours. Photographing someone in traditional dress without asking, following them for a shot, or blocking a path for a photograph are all behaviors that have become serious enough to prompt legal responses in some areas. In all other contexts: use judgment, ask when in doubt, and take no for an answer.

title: Japan Still Runs on Cash 窶・Here窶冱 How Much to Bring and Where to Get It
excerpt: Cards are increasingly accepted in Japan, but 窶亙ncreasingly窶・is not the same as 窶腕eliably.窶・Plan for cash and treat the card acceptance as a bonus.
heroImageAlt: A hand holding Japanese yen banknotes in front of a temple market
author: TABI Editorial

sections.heading: Why Japan still uses cash
paragraphs: Japan窶冱 cash culture is not technological backwardness. It reflects a combination of historical distrust of credit, very low crime rates that make carrying cash safe, a retail infrastructure built around cash transactions, and a cultural preference for precise payments. The ﾂ･100, ﾂ･500, and ﾂ･1,000 denominations handle most daily transactions efficiently. Japan also has one of the lowest cash crime rates in the world 窶・losing a wallet in Tokyo has a high probability of recovery.

sections.heading: Is it changing
paragraphs: Yes, slowly. Since 2020, card acceptance has expanded significantly 窶・major chains (convenience stores, department stores, chain restaurants) almost universally accept Visa and Mastercard now. But neighborhood restaurants, small temple admission counters, some bus routes, onsen entrance fees, market stalls, and many rural businesses remain cash-only. You will encounter cash-only situations, and the choice of whether to have cash available is not really a choice.

sections.heading: How much to bring
paragraphs: A reasonable daily cash budget for someone eating well and visiting paid attractions is ﾂ･5,000窶・,000 per day. For a one-week trip to Tokyo and Kyoto, ﾂ･50,000窶・0,000 in cash is a workable target, supplemented by card use where available. Carry ﾂ･10,000窶・0,000 on you at any given time 窶・enough for a day窶冱 expenses with a buffer. More than ﾂ･30,000 in a wallet is unnecessary given the ATM density in Japan.

sections.heading: The 7-Eleven ATM
paragraphs: The most reliable cash source for foreign visitors: 7-Bank ATMs at 7-Eleven convenience stores. These accept almost all international cards, have English menus, charge a flat fee (around ﾂ･110窶・20 depending on your bank), and are open 24 hours. Japan Post ATMs (in post offices) and some AEON bank ATMs are also reliable. Traditional Japanese bank ATMs 窶・Mizuho, MUFG, SMBC 窶・frequently do not accept foreign cards or only accept them during banking hours. Use the convenience store ATMs.

sections.heading: Where cards are now accepted
paragraphs: Cards work reliably at: hotel check-in and checkout, department stores and major shopping centers, chain restaurants (Yoshinoya, Sukiya, many ramen chains), most supermarkets, Shinkansen ticket machines (for online booking pickup), pharmacy chains, and tourist attractions in major cities. Cards work unreliably or not at all at: local izakaya, neighborhood restaurants, temples and shrines, market stalls, some local buses, small guesthouses, and anything described as 窶惑amily run.窶・When you see a menu without a payment logo visible, assume cash.

sections.heading: The IC card exception
paragraphs: The IC card (Suica or ICOCA) sits between cash and card and is worth treating separately. It functions as a prepaid card accepted on trains, buses, and at convenience stores, and it can be loaded at any JR ticket machine with cash. Load it from a 7-Eleven ATM withdrawal, use it everywhere it窶冱 accepted, and keep ﾂ･2,000+ on it at all times. The combination of an IC card and a cash reserve handles 95% of daily transactions without any friction.

title: SIM Card vs Pocket Wi-Fi in Japan: What Nobody Tells You Before You Choose
excerpt: Both work. One is significantly more convenient. The answer has changed in the last three years and most travel articles haven窶冲 caught up.
heroImageAlt: A person holding a phone with a Japan map open in a busy train station
author: TABI Editorial

sections.heading: Why you need internet
paragraphs: Navigation in Japan without internet access is not impossible 窶・the signage in major train stations is multilingual and the numbered exit system at Tokyo Metro stations is excellent 窶・but it is significantly harder. Google Maps with Japanese public transit data is the most useful single tool available to a visitor, and it requires a live connection to function well. Sort this before you arrive.

sections.heading: Pocket Wi-Fi: the case for
paragraphs: A pocket Wi-Fi is a small device you carry that creates a personal Wi-Fi hotspot using a Japanese mobile network. The main advantages: it can connect multiple devices simultaneously (useful if you窶决e traveling with a partner or need a tablet for navigation), it doesn窶冲 require changing your phone窶冱 SIM or settings, and it窶冱 been the standard solution for visitors for years 窶・airport rental desks are efficient and the return process is a prepaid envelope drop.

sections.heading: Pocket Wi-Fi: the case against
paragraphs: It窶冱 one more thing to carry and charge. The battery typically lasts 8窶・0 hours under normal use, which is not a full travel day. You need to remember it. If you leave it at the hotel, you have no internet until you get back. The data may be throttled after 3窶・GB per day depending on the provider, which matters if you窶决e using video navigation or uploading photos.

sections.heading: SIM cards
paragraphs: A Japanese tourist SIM replaces your existing SIM and gives you a Japanese data connection. Data-only SIMs (no calls, no texts) run about ﾂ･3,000窶・,000 for 15窶・0 days with 5窶・0GB. Voice SIMs are more expensive. The advantages: you carry nothing extra, battery life is your phone窶冱 own, and you窶决e always connected. The disadvantages: you need to physically swap SIMs, store your original one safely, and configure the APN settings (which take 5 minutes and require looking up your provider).

sections.heading: eSIM: the best option now
paragraphs: If your phone supports eSIM (most phones released after 2020 do), a Japanese eSIM is the cleanest solution. You download the profile before you arrive, activate it at the airport, and your phone connects to a Japanese network while keeping your original SIM active for calls and texts from home. No physical swap, no extra device, no configuration beyond installing the profile. Providers like IIJmio, Ubigi, Airalo, and several others offer Japan eSIMs starting around $15窶・5 USD for 7窶・0 days. Check compatibility before you buy.

sections.heading: Where to get them
paragraphs: Pocket Wi-Fi rental counters are at Narita, Haneda, Kansai, and most other major airports. Booking in advance online is cheaper than walk-up rental. Physical SIMs are sold at airport convenience stores and electronics retailers (Bic Camera, Yodobashi). eSIMs are purchased online before departure 窶・activate them when you land. For a trip of 5窶・4 days with a single traveler, the eSIM or a basic tourist SIM is the practical recommendation.

title: The Only Japanese Phrases You Actually Need
excerpt: Japanese is difficult. A handful of phrases isn窶冲. The ten expressions below will handle the majority of situations a visitor encounters, and they take about 20 minutes to learn.
heroImageAlt: A Japanese menu board with handwritten chalk specials at a small restaurant
author: TABI Editorial

sections.heading: Why bother
paragraphs: English in Japan is available, patchily. Major chain restaurants have English menus or picture menus. Train stations in Tokyo and Kyoto are multilingual. Hotel staff at international properties speak English. Beyond these contexts 窶・at the neighborhood izakaya, the market stall, the onsen town窶冱 local restaurant 窶・Japanese is what you have. A few phrases, used with reasonable pronunciation and a willingness to repeat them, will get you through almost everything. Nobody expects fluency. They do notice the attempt.

sections.heading: The four daily essentials
paragraphs: Sumimasen 窶・excuse me, sorry to bother you. The Swiss Army knife of Japanese phrases. Use it to get attention in a restaurant, to apologize for bumping into someone, to start a question, to ask for help. Arigatou gozaimasu 窶・thank you very much. The formal version. Useful for anything beyond casual thanks. Hai 窶・yes. More affirmative than it translates; also used to acknowledge that you heard something. Ie / iie 窶・no. Softer than it sounds. These four phrases, repeated calmly and clearly, handle a remarkable proportion of basic daily interactions.

sections.heading: At restaurants
paragraphs: Kore wo kudasai 窶・this please. Point at the menu item. Three words, works everywhere. Hitotsu / futatsu / mittsu 窶・one / two / three. Ordering quantities. O-kaikei kudasai 窶・the bill please. You can also do the universal rectangular-finger-gesture (tracing a rectangle in the air with both index fingers) and everyone understands. Oishii 窶・delicious. Using this mid-meal in a small restaurant will reliably cause the chef to emerge from the kitchen.

sections.heading: Getting around
paragraphs: [ Destination ] wa doko desu ka? 窶・Where is [ destination ]? Fill in the location name. Show the name written down and follow this phrase with the map on your phone and you will receive directions, usually very thorough directions, sometimes with an escort. Chotto matte kudasai 窶・please wait a moment. For when someone is talking faster than you can process.

sections.heading: Shopping
paragraphs: Ikura desu ka? 窶・How much is it? Most shops will simply type the price on a calculator or show you the price tag, but the question opens the interaction correctly. Kore wa nan desu ka? 窶・What is this? For menus and market stalls where the item is unidentifiable. Expect a demonstration.

sections.heading: Pronunciation
paragraphs: Japanese vowels are consistent: A is 窶和h,窶・I is 窶脇e,窶・U is 窶椀o,窶・E is 窶脇h,窶・O is 窶椀h.窶・They don窶冲 shift the way English vowels do. 窶連rigatou窶・is pronounced ah-ree-gah-toh, not ah-rig-uh-toe. Getting the vowels right matters more than anything else and takes five minutes of practice. Japanese people will meet you much more than halfway on pronunciation as long as the vowels are approximately correct.

title: The Tokyo Subway: How to Navigate It Without Getting Lost
excerpt: Tokyo窶冱 train network is the most complex urban rail system in the world. It is also, once you understand the basics, extremely easy to use.
heroImageAlt: The platform at Shinjuku Station during morning rush hour with commuters boarding a train
author: TABI Editorial

sections.heading: Two networks, one system
paragraphs: Tokyo窶冱 rail network is operated by multiple companies, of which two matter most to visitors: Tokyo Metro (13 lines, run by Tokyo Metro Co.) and Toei Subway (4 lines, run by the Tokyo Metropolitan Government). They connect at major stations, but they are separate systems with separate fares 窶・transferring between them costs extra unless you have an IC card, which handles the fare calculation automatically. On an IC card, the transfer cost is minimal and seamless. On a paper ticket, you窶冤l need to buy a new one each time. Get the IC card.

sections.heading: The Yamanote Line
paragraphs: The Yamanote Line is not a subway 窶・it窶冱 a JR surface rail loop that runs around central Tokyo, connecting Shinjuku, Shibuya, Harajuku, Ebisu, Osaki, Shinagawa, Tokyo, Akihabara, Ueno, Nippori, Ikebukuro, and back. It takes about an hour to complete the full loop. Think of it as your outer ring: when you want to move between major neighborhoods, this is usually how you do it. Frequency during peak hours is every two to three minutes.

sections.heading: IC card vs paper ticket
paragraphs: Buy a Suica IC card at any JR ticket machine at the airport or a major station. Load it with ﾂ･2,000窶・,000 to start. Tap in, tap out, and the fare is deducted automatically. You never need to calculate fares or buy destination-specific tickets. A paper ticket requires you to select the exact destination and correct fare before you board, which requires reading a map that is entirely in Japanese at some smaller stations. There is no scenario where a paper ticket is preferable to an IC card.

sections.heading: Reading the maps
paragraphs: Tokyo Metro maps are color-coded by line and numbered by station. Each line has a letter prefix and each station a number (e.g., G07 for Ginza on the Ginza line). This number system is displayed on platform signs and inside carriages, which means you can navigate without reading kanji at all. The printed maps at train stations are free and multi-language. Inside the carriages, digital displays above the doors show the next station in Japanese, English, Chinese, and Korean.

sections.heading: Google Maps
paragraphs: Google Maps with transit mode is accurate and reliable for Tokyo public transport. Enter your destination, select the transit option, and it will show you the correct line, platform, number of stops, and transfer instructions. The journey planner also shows fare estimates. Use it as your primary navigation tool. Japan-specific apps like HyperDia and Navitime are more detailed for complex Shinkansen bookings but offer nothing additional for basic subway navigation.

sections.heading: The last train
paragraphs: Tokyo窶冱 trains stop running around midnight (times vary by line and station; check Google Maps for the exact last train). This is not like New York or London where the service continues through the night with reduced frequency. The trains stop, full stop. Taxis are available but expensive after midnight and in short supply on Friday and Saturday nights. If you窶决e out late, plan your route in advance and know your last train time. Getting stranded at midnight in Shibuya is solvable but inconvenient and expensive.

title: What You Can and Cannot Photograph in Japan
excerpt: Japan is exceptionally photogenic. It also has specific places and situations where photography is restricted, disrespectful, or now actively prohibited. Knowing the difference matters.
heroImageAlt: A photographer framing a traditional Japanese street scene at dawn with no other people visible
author: TABI Editorial

sections.heading: The general principle
paragraphs: In most outdoor public spaces in Japan, photography is unrestricted. Streets, parks, temple grounds, shrine pathways, market areas, train stations 窶・all are freely photographable. The restrictions apply inside specific buildings, in areas where signage prohibits it, and in situations where the photography causes problems for other people or for the place itself. Read the signs, watch what other people are doing, and when in doubt, ask.

sections.heading: Inside temples and shrines
paragraphs: Interior photography inside the main hall of a temple or shrine is usually prohibited. The sign will typically show a camera with a red line through it. Some places allow photography inside with a fee; some famous main halls (Kinkaku-ji窶冱 interior, Todai-ji窶冱 Buddha) are not accessible at all. Gardens attached to temples are generally freely photographable. Detailed garden photography is expected 窶・it窶冱 one of the primary reasons people visit.

sections.heading: At onsen and public baths
paragraphs: Photography is prohibited at onsen and sento (public baths), including in the changing rooms. This is absolute and enforced. Phones are not permitted in the bathing areas of most facilities, and taking your phone into a bathing area is both a rule violation and a privacy invasion of every other person present. Leave the phone in the locker.

sections.heading: Photographing strangers
paragraphs: Japan doesn窶冲 have a specific law against street photography, but the social norms around photographing strangers are similar to those in most countries: pointing a camera directly at someone窶冱 face without consent, following someone for a photograph, or photographing children without parental consent all cross reasonable lines. Most Japanese people will not object verbally, but this reflects a cultural reluctance to confront rather than permission.

sections.heading: In Gion, Kyoto
paragraphs: Gion and the surrounding geisha districts of Kyoto are a specific and documented problem. Years of tourists surrounding and following maiko and geiko for photographs has led to complaints from residents, businesses, and the performers themselves. Some streets now have photography restriction signs during evening hours. Obeying these is not optional 窶・local authorities have the ability to issue fines. More simply: if you see a maiko walking to an appointment, she is not a photo opportunity. She is a person doing her job.

sections.heading: On trains
paragraphs: Photography on trains is not explicitly prohibited but is not commonly practiced for good reason: photographing other passengers without consent in an enclosed space they cannot leave is uncomfortable regardless of country. Photographing the view from the window is universally fine. Photographing your own group is fine. Pointing a camera at strangers in a carriage is not.

title: Your First Ryokan: What to Expect, What to Do, and What Not to Wear
excerpt: A traditional Japanese inn is not a hotel. The sooner you accept that, the better the stay.
heroImageAlt: A traditional ryokan room with tatami floor, low table, and garden view
author: TABI Editorial

sections.heading: Check-in, checkout, and why timing matters
paragraphs: The check-in time at a ryokan is usually 3pm and checkout is 10am. These aren't suggestions. Your room is being transformed twice a day — futon laid out at turndown, rolled away before breakfast — and that choreography is coordinated with dinner and bath times. Arriving at 6pm and expecting your room to be ready the way a hotel room is always ready will result in confusion on both sides.

sections.heading: Shoes, slippers, and one rule about the toilet
paragraphs: When you arrive, you remove your shoes at the entrance and switch into slippers. The slippers go everywhere except tatami rooms — in tatami rooms you go barefoot or in socks. There will be a separate pair of toilet slippers in the bathroom. Put them on to enter, take them off to leave. Forgetting and wandering the hallway in toilet slippers is the ryokan equivalent of walking through a dinner party in a life jacket.

sections.heading: Your room
paragraphs: Your room will have: a low table, zabuton cushions, a tokonoma (a small alcove, usually with a seasonal arrangement), a yukata folded somewhere, and a kettle with tea. The yukata is your uniform. Wear it to the onsen, to dinner if the place is traditional, back to your room. It's a cotton robe, not a costume. Left side over right — right over left is how the dead are dressed.

sections.heading: The onsen
paragraphs: The onsen, if there is one, operates on a schedule — typically gender-segregated, sometimes with rotating access to different baths. The rules: wash thoroughly at the shower station before entering the communal water. Don't bring a towel into the bath. The small hand towel goes on your head or beside the tub. Don't get in if you've been drinking heavily — people faint, and it's embarrassing for everyone.

sections.heading: Kaiseki dinner
paragraphs: Dinner is kaiseki — a multi-course meal built around seasonal ingredients. It arrives in a sequence you don't choose and can't rush. Don't ask for the menu in advance. Don't request variations unless you have a genuine allergy. The meal takes 90 minutes to two hours, the plates will be small, and you will feel, briefly, like you're not getting enough food. You are getting enough food. The point is rhythm.

sections.heading: Breakfast
paragraphs: Breakfast follows the same logic: rice, miso soup, pickled vegetables, grilled fish, tofu, a small egg dish. It arrives at a fixed time, usually between 7:30 and 9am. It is also the best breakfast in the world, and I say this as someone who is not generally enthusiastic about breakfast.

sections.heading: What it costs
paragraphs: Prices run from around ¥8,000 per person at a decent mid-range option with onsen and breakfast to ¥80,000+ per person at the higher end with full kaiseki. The sweet spot is in the ¥15,000–¥25,000 range — particularly in onsen towns like Kinosaki Onsen in Hyogo or Gero in Gifu, which don't have the premium of Hakone or Kyoto but are genuinely excellent.

sections.heading: On language
paragraphs: A final note on language. The staff will likely not speak much English, and that's genuinely fine. The experience requires very little language to work. 'Arigatou gozaimasu' (thank you very much) and 'oishii' (delicious) will handle 80% of situations. The rest comes from gestures, patience, and the fact that the people running a ryokan have been doing this for decades and know how to communicate without words.

title: Why January Is the Best Month to Visit Japan (Yes, Really)
excerpt: No cherry blossoms. No 38-degree humidity. No crowds. January is when Japan stops performing for tourists and just exists.
heroImageAlt: Snow-dusted temple grounds in Kyoto in winter
author: TABI Editorial

sections.heading: Why even consider January
paragraphs: Summer in Japan is spectacular and exhausting. Cherry blossom season is magical and so crowded that the famous spots feel like theme parks. Autumn foliage is genuinely among the most beautiful things in the natural world and is also, by October, completely overrun. January has none of these problems, because January has a reputation for cold and grey that is only partly deserved.

sections.heading: The actual weather
paragraphs: The actual weather in Tokyo in January: between 4°C and 10°C during the day, clear skies more often than you'd expect, occasional rain, rare snow. Not harsh. A good wool coat is enough. Kyoto gets occasional snow — which on temple grounds with grey stones and winter moss looks exactly like the photograph you've seen your whole life, except there are almost no other people in it.

sections.heading: Where the crowds went
paragraphs: What January removes: the tourist surge disappears after New Year's (January 1–3), when domestic travelers return home and the holiday-season international visitors are gone. From January 5th onwards, you can visit Kinkaku-ji without being engulfed in 2,000 people. You can walk the Arashiyama bamboo grove with no one in your frame. This is not an exaggeration. On a cold January Tuesday, the path is genuinely empty.

sections.heading: New Year rituals
paragraphs: What January adds: the rituals of New Year's — hatsumode, the first shrine visit of the year — give the first week of January a feeling of purposefulness that ordinary months don't have. Major shrines are packed January 1–3 and quieter but still festive through the first week. Asakusa's Senso-ji at 6am on January 2nd is one of the best crowds in the world to be part of.

sections.heading: Onsen towns in winter
paragraphs: Onsen towns are at their best in winter, and this is not a small thing. The specific pleasure of sitting in an outdoor rotenburo — a hot spring bath — with snow falling and steam rising is not available in July. Hakone from Tokyo, Kinosaki Onsen from Kyoto, Beppu in Kyushu: these are all better in winter on nearly every metric. The baths are hotter, the air is colder, and the bowl of food you eat afterwards is twice as satisfying.

sections.heading: Skiing in Hokkaido
paragraphs: Ski resorts: Japan has some of the best powder snow in the world. Hokkaido specifically — Niseko, Furano, Rusutsu — has developed into a serious international ski destination, partly because the light dry snow that falls there is genuinely exceptional. January is peak season. If skiing is relevant to your trip, January in Hokkaido is not a consolation prize. It's the main event.

sections.heading: Flights, hotels, and prices
paragraphs: The practical case: flights cost less in January than in October or April. Hotels cost less. Popular restaurants with months-long waiting lists have more availability. The ryokan in Hakone that has no rooms in November has rooms in January, often at a meaningfully lower rate.

sections.heading: What winter reveals
paragraphs: What Japan offers in winter that it can't offer in spring or autumn: something closer to daily life. When a place is over-visited, it optimizes for visitors. When it's not being watched, it relaxes. January Japan is commuters and schoolchildren and the ramen shop that opens at 6:30am for the people who work early shifts. That Japan is worth seeing, and it's more accessible than people think.

title: The Convenience Store Is Japan's Greatest Culinary Achievement
excerpt: Seven-Eleven in Japan is not Seven-Eleven anywhere else. This distinction matters and deserves your full attention.
heroImageAlt: Onigiri and sandwiches at a Japanese convenience store counter
author: TABI Editorial

sections.heading: This is not convenience store food
paragraphs: The first thing to understand about Japanese convenience stores — konbini — is that the food is genuinely good. Not 'good for a convenience store' good. Just good. The onigiri at 7-Eleven is made fresh every morning. The egg salad sandwich at Lawson is produced with a specific grade of bread and a specific ratio of egg to mayonnaise that has been optimized through decades of iteration. The hot foods at Family Mart are cooked to a standard that most fast-casual restaurants in other countries don't meet.

sections.heading: What konbini do beyond food
paragraphs: Konbini are everywhere. In Tokyo, you're never more than a three-minute walk from one. They're open 24 hours. They sell alcohol until midnight or all night depending on the prefecture. They have ATMs that accept foreign cards — the 7-Bank machines at 7-Eleven are the most consistently reliable. They sell stamps, ship packages, pay utility bills, and print documents from a USB drive. They are infrastructure, and understanding them is understanding how Japan works.

sections.heading: Onigiri
paragraphs: What to eat. Onigiri are rice balls with a filling — tuna mayo, pickled plum, salmon, bonito — wrapped in a three-panel system that keeps the seaweed crispy until you open it. The opening system is printed on the packet with numbered arrows. Follow them. Don't try to unwrap it another way. You will end up with rice everywhere and no dignified explanation.

sections.heading: The egg salad sandwich
paragraphs: The egg salad sandwich (tamago sando) at 7-Eleven specifically has developed a following that crosses national lines. The egg is thick-cut and slightly sweet. The bread is soft in the particular way that soft white bread in Japan is soft, which is different from anywhere else and difficult to explain. Buy one on your first morning and the question of why people travel to Japan specifically for the food will answer itself.

sections.heading: Hot food
paragraphs: Hot food: the oden pot is a winter staple — simmering broth with fish cakes, tofu, daikon, and boiled eggs. You pick items with tongs and the staff counts them into your bill. Steamed buns (nikuman) live in the hot case near the register and are excellent at 11pm when nothing else is open. The Family Mart chicken has been the subject of genuine national debate about whether it surpasses McDonald's. The answer depends on the outlet and the day.

sections.heading: Drinks
paragraphs: Drinks: canned coffee in Japan is a separate category of human achievement. Boss Coffee and the konbini own-brand cans have been developed through R&D cycles that most car companies would find excessive. Around ¥120 a can, available cold or hot depending on the season. The hot canned coffee on a November morning in Tokyo is one of those sensory memories that travel writers try to describe and never quite manage.

sections.heading: Budget and how to order
paragraphs: Practical notes: budget ¥500–700 for a full konbini meal. Pay at the register. Staff will ask 'atatame masuka?' (would you like this warmed up?) for eligible items. Yes is the answer. There will also be a question about a bag — a small fee applies, but it's worth it. Don't eat inside the store if it can be avoided; there's usually a counter or seats outside.

title: Tokyo's Kissaten: The Coffee Shops That Refuse to Change
excerpt: Somewhere between the third-wave cafés and the chain stores, Tokyo's old coffee shops have been quietly waiting.
heroImageAlt: A dimly lit kissaten coffee shop interior with wooden counters and vintage cups
author: TABI Editorial

sections.heading: What kissaten means
paragraphs: The word kissaten has no clean English translation. A coffee shop, technically. But that misses the point. A kissaten is a room where time moves differently — where the owner has been making the same blend since 1978, where the cups are heavier than they need to be, where nobody is on a laptop, and where the ashtrays are still on the tables even if nobody uses them anymore.

sections.heading: Why they are invisible to visitors
paragraphs: Tokyo has thousands of them. Most are invisible to visitors because they don't appear on Instagram, they rarely have English menus, and the doors look like they open onto second-floor landings or basements with no visible signage. This is not an accident. Kissaten are not trying to attract new customers. They already have their customers, and those customers have been coming in for decades.

sections.heading: Where to find them
paragraphs: The ones worth finding are usually in older neighborhoods — Koenji, Shimokitazawa, Yanaka, the parts of Shinjuku that Airbnb hasn't reached yet. They cluster near train stations that aren't on the tourist circuit. The right strategy is to walk until you see a menu in the window that was laminated in approximately 1990.

sections.heading: What you are drinking
paragraphs: What you're drinking is blend coffee — filter or drip coffee made with a proprietary blend the owner has been refining for decades. This is not specialty coffee in the third-wave sense. It's not trying to taste of blueberries or dried apricot. It tastes of coffee, deeply, without fuss. Served black by default, with a small jug of cream on the side.

sections.heading: The morning set
paragraphs: Food at a kissaten tends to be one of two things: a morning set or toast. The morning set, usually available until 11am, is a cup of coffee plus a thick slice of toast with butter and jam — sometimes with a small boiled egg on the side — for ¥400 to ¥600. It is one of the great underrated breakfasts in the world, and it is not something you will find on any list of Tokyo's best breakfasts.

sections.heading: How to sit properly
paragraphs: Sitting in a kissaten requires a different pace. You don't order at a counter. You sit down, wait for the owner to come to you, and order quietly. Then you wait. The coffee takes longer to arrive than you'd expect, and you will feel, briefly, uncomfortable with the silence. That feeling passes. That's the point.

sections.heading: Three worth knowing in Tokyo
paragraphs: A few worth knowing in Tokyo: Café de l'Ambre in Ginza (open since 1948; the owner worked there into his nineties), Chatei Hatou in Shibuya (precise and serious about the coffee), Kayaba Coffee in Yanaka (technically new, but in a building from 1938 that does the work). Don't go to all of them. Pick one, sit down properly, and stay for at least an hour.

sections.heading: What they represent
paragraphs: The reason kissaten deserve attention isn't nostalgia, exactly. It's that they represent a way of organizing time that has largely been optimized away. Nobody is tracking you. Nobody cares about table turnover. You ordered coffee and you're welcome to stay all morning. That this now feels unusual is its own kind of commentary.

title: Kanazawa: The City Most Japan Itineraries Skip — and Shouldn't
excerpt: Three hours from Tokyo by bullet train. A fraction of Kyoto's crowds. Arguably better seafood. There is no good reason it's on so few itineraries.
heroImageAlt: Higashi Chaya geisha district in Kanazawa, wooden ochaya buildings lining a quiet street
author: TABI Editorial

sections.heading: Why Kanazawa is overlooked
paragraphs: Kanazawa is on the wrong side of Japan. That seems to be the only explanation for why it receives a third of Kyoto's visitor numbers while having a castle, a geisha district, one of Japan's three great landscape gardens, and what most serious food people consider the best seafood market outside of Tokyo.

sections.heading: Getting there
paragraphs: The bullet train from Tokyo (the Hokuriku Shinkansen, extended in 2015) takes 2.5 hours. From Kyoto it's 2.5 to 3 hours with a transfer. Neither journey is difficult. The problem is psychological — most visitors arrive with a Tokyo-Kyoto-Osaka triangle already built into their itinerary, and Kanazawa sits outside of it, requiring a detour that most itineraries can't absorb.

sections.heading: Kenroku-en
paragraphs: Kenroku-en is the main draw, and correctly so. One of Japan's three great gardens — a designation given by Edo-period landscape critics — it was developed over two centuries by the ruling Maeda clan and covers 11.4 hectares. In cherry blossom season it's crowded. In late November, when the pine trees are wrapped in rope against the winter snow in a technique called yukizuri, it's extraordinary and very nearly empty.

sections.heading: Higashi Chaya
paragraphs: Higashi Chaya is Kanazawa's best-preserved geisha quarter and is smaller and less tourist-managed than Kyoto's Gion. Some of the ochaya (teahouses) are still operating. The street is wooden and narrow and hasn't changed structurally since the early 19th century. Come early morning or at dusk and it's yours.

sections.heading: The food
paragraphs: The food. Kanazawa sits on the Sea of Japan coast and receives fish from waters that don't reach the Pacific side in the same condition. The seafood at Omicho Market — a covered market the size of a small neighborhood — is some of the best in the country. In winter: crab, specifically the male snow crab called zuwaigani. In spring: firefly squid. Year-round: a sashimi set from any mid-range restaurant near the market will be better than most of what you'd eat in a Tokyo tourist district at twice the price.

sections.heading: Gold leaf and contemporary art
paragraphs: Kanazawa also has a serious craft tradition. Gold leaf production has been centered here since the Edo period — 99% of Japan's kinpaku (gold leaf) still comes from Kanazawa workshops. The '21st Century Museum of Contemporary Art' (designed by SANAA, open since 2004) is one of the better contemporary art museums in Japan and draws a local crowd, not just tourists.

sections.heading: Where to stay
paragraphs: Where to stay: the city center is small enough that almost anywhere is walkable. The area around Katamachi is convenient and has good restaurant density. Stay two nights minimum. The city rewards an unhurried second day in a way that a single overnight doesn't capture.

sections.heading: What Kanazawa has that Kyoto lost
paragraphs: What Kanazawa has that Kyoto has largely lost is the feeling of visiting somewhere people actually live. The geisha quarter isn't a theme park. The old town is genuinely old and genuinely in use. If you're looking for that — and increasingly, it's what travelers say they want — Kanazawa is close to the correct answer.

title: Tsukiji Outer Market: Where to Actually Eat (and What to Skip)
excerpt: The inner market moved to Toyosu in 2018. The outer market never moved and never needed to.
heroImageAlt: Fresh seafood stalls at Tsukiji outer market at early morning
author: TABI Editorial

sections.heading: What stayed and what moved
paragraphs: In 2018, the famous wholesale fish market at Tsukiji relocated operations to Toyosu, a reclaimed island further east. Travel writers immediately started writing elegies for Tsukiji. What they missed is that the part most visitors cared about — the outer market, with its 400-odd shops selling fresh seafood, street food, knives, and tamago-yaki — never went anywhere and shows no signs of going anywhere.

sections.heading: When to arrive
paragraphs: The outer market opens early. Most stalls are running by 6am. The target arrival window is between 7am and 10am, when the selection is widest and the food is freshest. By noon, the best stalls will have sold out of the things worth eating. Come for breakfast. This is not negotiable.

sections.heading: Sushi
paragraphs: What to eat, in order of importance. First: sushi from one of the small counters in the side streets off the main market lane. These are eight-seat affairs with a glass case of fish in front. Point at what you want, sit down, eat it immediately. The fish was at Toyosu wholesale market this morning. A ¥1,500–2,000 set will be the best sushi you've had in your life, and if you don't believe that until you're eating it, that's fine.

sections.heading: Tamagoyaki
paragraphs: Second: tamagoyaki, the thick-rolled sweet omelette that is specific to the Tsukiji style. The most famous vendors are Tamagoyaki-ya Shouro and the stalls along the Namiyoke Shrine side. They sell slices from the pan on wooden sticks. The outside is slightly caramelized. The inside is custard-soft. It costs around ¥200 and you will want two.

sections.heading: Ikura on rice
paragraphs: Third: ikura (salmon roe) on rice, from any stall that makes it fresh. The roe should be bright orange and very fresh — the texture is different from what you've likely had before. A small bowl is ¥500–800. It's one of those things that recalibrates your understanding of what fish can be.

sections.heading: What to skip
paragraphs: What to skip: the photographed seafood-on-skewer vendors near the main entrance that have been optimized for social media. The scallops are fine but priced for tourists, and the line is full of people taking the same photo. Walk past them, turn into the side streets, and find the stalls where nobody has their phone out.

sections.heading: The knife shops
paragraphs: The knife shops deserve a separate mention. Tsukiji has more professional knife dealers per square meter than anywhere outside of the knife-making cities. If you're planning to buy Japanese kitchen knives — and you should, they are among the best things to bring home from Japan — buy them here after handling them and talking to the vendor. The selection goes from ¥3,000 santoku knives for everyday home cooking to ¥80,000 custom yanagiba for the serious cook.

sections.heading: Getting there
paragraphs: Getting there: subway to Tsukijishijo (Oedo Line) or Tsukiji (Hibiya Line). Allow at least two hours. Three if you're serious about the knives.

title: How to Eat at an Izakaya Without Looking Completely Lost
excerpt: The curtain over the door. The laminated menu with pictures on maybe two pages. The small charge you didn't order. You'll be fine.
heroImageAlt: Inside a Tokyo izakaya with warm lighting, wooden tables and yakitori on the grill
author: TABI Editorial

sections.heading: What an izakaya is
paragraphs: An izakaya is a Japanese pub — not a restaurant, not a bar, but the thing that happens when the two concepts are combined without either one winning. You drink and eat in roughly equal measure. The food comes in small plates meant to be shared across the table. Nobody orders a main course. The lighting is low and the room is loud and you'll probably be sharing it with salarymen who have loosened their ties, which is always a good sign.

sections.heading: Ordering from the menu
paragraphs: Most izakaya have a printed menu and sometimes a laminated picture menu. Order from the picture menu if there is one. Point at what you want. Nobody will be offended. If there's no picture menu, say 'osusume wa nan desu ka?' — what do you recommend? Someone will point at something, and whatever they point at will be edible.

sections.heading: The opening round
paragraphs: The standard opening order: a round of beers (nama biru, draft, or a sawa cocktail made from shochu and soda), plus two or three small dishes to start. Edamame, karaage (fried chicken), and potato salad are almost universally available and almost universally good. They are not exciting. They are correct. Order them while you figure out the rest of the menu.

sections.heading: Otoshi: the charge you did not order
paragraphs: The small charge that appears on your bill without explanation: it's called otoshi. Almost every izakaya charges a per-person seating fee of ¥200–600, and in return brings a small snack you didn't order — sometimes pickled vegetables, sometimes a small salad, occasionally something genuinely excellent. It's not a scam. It's standard. The bill is correct.

sections.heading: What to eat
paragraphs: Order drinks first, then food, then more drinks, then more food — loosely. Good things to order beyond the basics: yakitori (grilled chicken on bamboo skewers — order mixed, specify 'tare' for sweet soy glaze or 'shio' for salt), dashimaki tamago (rolled omelette made with dashi broth, usually soft and fragrant), and sashimi if the place looks like it takes its fish seriously, which you can usually tell from whether there are locals at the bar eating it.

sections.heading: Shochu and chuhai
paragraphs: Shochu is likely what you're drinking in the cocktails and don't know it. Distilled most commonly from sweet potato (imo-jochu) or barley (mugi-jochu), it's served over ice or mixed with soda. Gentler than whisky, it goes with everything on the menu. If someone offers chuhai, that's shochu with fruit soda — the izakaya equivalent of a spritz, and perfectly fine.

sections.heading: Getting the bill
paragraphs: You leave when you've decided to leave. To get the bill, catch someone's eye and make a rectangular gesture with your index fingers — this is the universal 'o-kaikei' signal and everyone will understand it immediately. The total will likely be split evenly if you're with a group. This is called warikan. Don't try to calculate individual shares. It goes against the spirit of the thing.

sections.heading: The golden rule
paragraphs: The golden rule: the more local and less translated the menu, the better the food. The izakaya in a residential neighborhood near a train station that isn't on any tourist map will beat the one with an English sign near the temples every time. Your hotel is on the tourist circuit. The izakaya should not be.

title: The IC Card Is the Best Thing in Japan. Here's How to Actually Use It.
excerpt: You can survive Tokyo's train network without one. You just won't want to.
heroImageAlt: Commuter tapping a Suica IC card at a Tokyo train station gate
author: TABI Editorial

sections.heading: Get one at the airport
paragraphs: Before you leave the airport, find a ticket machine and get a Suica. Don't deliberate. Don't check whether you really need one. You need one.

sections.heading: What an IC card is
paragraphs: An IC card is a rechargeable transit card — the same basic technology as London's Oyster or Hong Kong's Octopus — except that in Japan it works on practically everything. Trains, subways, buses, ferries, taxis in major cities, convenience stores, vending machines, coin lockers, the self-checkout at Don Quijote at 2am. The list keeps growing.

sections.heading: Suica or ICOCA
paragraphs: Suica is issued by JR East and works in Tokyo and most of eastern Japan. ICOCA is the Kansai equivalent, issued by JR West, and works in Osaka, Kyoto, Kobe, and surrounding areas. The practical answer to which one you should get: they're fully interoperable. Get whichever one is available at the airport you land at. It genuinely doesn't matter.

sections.heading: Loading money
paragraphs: Loading money: find any green JR ticket machine, insert your card, press 'Charge' (there's an English option), and add ¥1,000, ¥2,000, or ¥5,000. Keep ¥2,000 or more on the card at all times. You don't want to be the person topping up at the fare gate while a queue builds behind you.

sections.heading: How tapping works
paragraphs: Tapping is the ritual that will define your first week. Tap the card on the reader as you enter the gate — a satisfying beep. Tap again as you leave — another beep, money deducted. A sad double-beep and a red light means insufficient balance. Step aside, top up at the nearest machine, which will be approximately five meters away.

sections.heading: Vending machines
paragraphs: One thing nobody mentions: the card works in vending machines too. Just tap the reader on the front panel. It's faster than coins, works every time, and makes carrying change feel archaic. After a week in Japan you'll find physical coins faintly offensive.

sections.heading: Apple Pay and Google Pay
paragraphs: Increasingly, you can also link Suica to Apple Pay or Google Pay. Setup takes about ten minutes and is worth doing if your phone supports it — you can top up from the app, which means you never need to find a machine. The setup process is in English on both platforms.

sections.heading: When you leave Japan
paragraphs: When you leave Japan: keep the card. It doesn't expire, and having it ready for your next trip is genuinely convenient. If you want to return it, do so at a JR ticket office — you'll get back your remaining balance minus a ¥220 handling fee. If you have less than ¥300 left, just keep it.

sections.heading: Why it matters
paragraphs: The larger point is this: the IC card makes it possible to navigate Tokyo's 130-plus stations within the Yamanote Line loop without ever buying a specific-destination ticket from a machine. That is not a trivial thing. It removes a class of friction that makes many transit systems — especially large Japanese ones — intimidating for first-time visitors. Get the card at the airport. Everything else is easier.

title: Japanese Whisky: What's Worth Buying and What's Just Marketing
excerpt: The golden age is over but the whisky is still exceptional. You just need to know what you're actually looking at.
heroImageAlt: Japanese whisky bottles displayed at a specialist liquor shop in Tokyo
author: TABI Editorial

sections.heading: The golden age and the scarcity
paragraphs: Somewhere around 2014, the world decided Japanese whisky was the best in the world. Suntory's Yamazaki 12 Year won a prominent international award. Whisky writers made pilgrimages to Nikka's distillery in Hokkaido. By 2016 you couldn't find a bottle of Yamazaki Single Malt for under ¥15,000 in a Tokyo duty-free. By 2020 you couldn't find it at all.

sections.heading: Why availability is slowly improving
paragraphs: The scarcity problem is real but overstated. What happened: global demand exploded, Japanese distilleries — which had been producing at a domestic-consumption pace — couldn't scale fast enough. The aged expressions ran dry. Secondary market prices went insane. But the distilleries kept working, and the whisky that was aging in 2016 and 2018 is now maturing. Availability is slowly improving.

sections.heading: What is genuinely worth buying
paragraphs: What's genuinely worth buying in 2026. Nikka From The Barrel: a blended whisky that consistently outperforms its price. Around ¥3,000 at a convenience store if you're lucky enough to find it. Suntory Toki: an affordable blend designed for highballs — order it at a bar before you commit to a bottle, which will take about one sip. Any single malt expression from Yamazaki or Yoichi that you can find at close to retail price rather than secondary market markup.

sections.heading: The highball format
paragraphs: The highball is the format. Japanese whisky culture is built around the whisky highball — spirit over ice with soda, served in a tall frosted glass. It sounds humble. It isn't. Order one at any decent whisky bar before you start shopping; it will calibrate your sense of what you're buying.

sections.heading: Where to buy
paragraphs: Where to buy: department store basement floors (depachika) and specialist liquor stores have the best selection and prices. Airport duty-free is convenient but the offering has narrowed significantly since the shortage began. Avoid hotel gift shops. The markup is severe and the selection is whatever moves fastest with tourists.

sections.heading: On labeling
paragraphs: On labeling: 'Japanese whisky' now has legal standards, introduced in 2021. The spirit must be made in Japan from Japanese ingredients, malted and aged in Japan, and bottled in Japan. Before this regulation, some brands were blending imported Scottish or Canadian spirit and calling it Japanese. The rules now require transparency. If a bottle says 'blended' without a specific distillery name, read the fine print.

sections.heading: What you will actually find
paragraphs: What you're most likely to actually find and afford: Suntory Kakubin (yellow label, a classic blend, excellent for highballs, around ¥1,200 at most liquor shops), Nikka Coffey Malt or Coffey Grain (made in a Coffey still, unusual and worth the ¥7,000), and possibly Chichibu expressions from Ichiro's Malt if you're prepared to spend ¥15,000 or more on something genuinely scarce.

sections.heading: One bottle to look for
paragraphs: One bottle to look for specifically: Mars Komagatake, from the highest-altitude distillery in Japan, located in Nagano. Production is limited and domestic allocation is small, but it shows up in specialist shops and occasionally at airport duty-free. It's not as famous as Yamazaki, but the gap in quality doesn't justify the gap in price and availability. Find it while that's still true.

title: Naoshima Is Japan's Art Island — and Nobody Tells You How to Do It Right
excerpt: It's a real island with real people living on it. The art just happens to be everywhere.
heroImageAlt: The yellow pumpkin sculpture by Yayoi Kusama on the coast of Naoshima
author: TABI Editorial

sections.heading: What Naoshima is
paragraphs: Naoshima sits in the Seto Inland Sea between Okayama and Takamatsu, about an hour by ferry from either city. It's small enough to cross on a rented bicycle in an afternoon. The population is around 3,000. The island has more Monets per square kilometer than most museums in Europe, which is a fact that sounds invented but isn't.

sections.heading: How it became what it is
paragraphs: The backstory: in the 1980s and 90s, the Benesse Corporation commissioned architect Tadao Ando to build a museum on a largely depopulated island. What followed over the next three decades was a collaboration between art, architecture, and landscape that turned Naoshima into something that shouldn't quite work but absolutely does. It's been widely imitated. Nothing else is quite like it.

sections.heading: The Chichu Art Museum
paragraphs: The main venues. The Chichu Art Museum is underground — no exterior visible from above, all natural light coming through skylights — and contains exactly three artists: Monet (five late Water Lilies), James Turrell (light installations), and Walter De Maria. That's all. The ticket is ¥2,100 and it is worth the trip from Tokyo to stand in the Monet room, which is a thing I don't say lightly.

sections.heading: The Art House Project
paragraphs: The Art House Project is what separates Naoshima from every other art destination in Japan. Artists were given abandoned houses in Honmura village and told to do what they wanted. James Turrell built a light installation into a farmhouse that you enter barefoot and lie on the floor of. Hiroshi Sugimoto turned a shrine into something else. These aren't galleries with labels and benches. They're encounters.

sections.heading: Getting there
paragraphs: Getting there from Tokyo is a half-day commitment minimum. Bullet train to Okayama, 20 minutes by local train to Uno port, 20-minute ferry to Naoshima. From Takamatsu it's about an hour by ferry. The journey filters out casual visitors, which is exactly why the island still has the quality it has.

sections.heading: Stay overnight
paragraphs: Stay overnight if you can. After the day-trip visitors leave on the last ferry, the island changes. The Art House Project buildings glow from inside. The grounds around Benesse House are open and quiet. You'll be one of a small number of people who aren't locals. This is an unusual experience in Japan and should be used properly — walk around, eat at the small restaurant near the port, sit somewhere without your phone.

sections.heading: On bicycles
paragraphs: On bicycles: the island is hilly and summer heat is serious. Electric bikes are available at the ferry terminal and are not cheating. The climb between Honmura and the Benesse area in August on a regular bicycle is not a good time.

sections.heading: The right mindset
paragraphs: Naoshima rewards a particular mindset: willingness to be disoriented, to find things with no label, to sit with something that doesn't explain itself. If that's what you came to Japan for — and increasingly, it is — the island earns its reputation. If you're looking for informational plaques and a gift shop at every turn, the Benesse Foundation apologizes in advance.

title: The JR Pass: Who It's For, Who It Isn't, and How to Not Overpay
excerpt: The JR Pass is one of Japan's most famous travel products. It's also, for a lot of itineraries, a waste of money.
heroImageAlt: Shinkansen bullet train at a Japanese station platform
author: TABI Editorial

sections.heading: The cost
paragraphs: The 7-day JR Pass costs ¥50,000 (around $330 USD at current rates). For that price you get unlimited travel on most JR-operated trains, including the Shinkansen, for one week. The 14-day version is ¥80,000. If your itinerary involves Tokyo, Kyoto, Osaka, and Hiroshima in that sequence, the math works. If your itinerary is mostly in one city with a few day trips, it almost certainly doesn't.

sections.heading: Break-even: when the pass wins
paragraphs: The break-even calculation for the 7-day pass: a single Tokyo–Kyoto Shinkansen (Hikari) ticket is ¥13,870 each way. Two of those is ¥27,740. Add Kyoto–Osaka (¥1,420), Osaka–Hiroshima (¥5,720 each way), a couple of JR local trips, and you're somewhere near ¥60,000. The pass wins. If you're only doing Tokyo–Kyoto round-trip with no other long-distance travel, a single round-trip costs ¥27,740 against the pass price of ¥50,000. You lose ¥22,260.

sections.heading: Nozomi vs Hikari
paragraphs: The JR Pass does not cover the Nozomi or Mizuho (the fastest Shinkansen), only the Hikari and Kodama. Tokyo to Kyoto on a Hikari takes about 2h45m versus 2h15m on a Nozomi. This is not a big difference and most people don't notice it.

sections.heading: What the pass covers that people miss
paragraphs: What the pass does cover that people underestimate: the Narita Express (N'EX) from the airport, the local JR lines within Tokyo (which serve some neighborhoods that subway doesn't), the JR Haruka from Kansai Airport into Kyoto, and most Shinkansen within Kyushu (on the Kyushu JR Pass). If you're starting from Narita, the N'EX alone is ¥3,070 to Shinjuku. Over seven days that's two trips or ¥6,140 saved before you board a Shinkansen.

sections.heading: Alternatives to the JR Pass
paragraphs: Alternatives to consider: IC cards (Suica) cover everything within cities. Individual Shinkansen tickets bought in advance can be slightly discounted through JR's Ekinet system. The Osaka Amazing Pass and the Kyoto Sightseeing Card cover local transit within those cities and don't require a JR Pass to function alongside them.

sections.heading: If you have decided to buy one
paragraphs: If you've already decided to buy the pass: buy it before leaving your home country. It used to be only available abroad, and while you can now buy it in Japan, the domestic price is roughly 10–20% higher. Get it from JR's official website or an authorized travel agent in your country. Activate it at a JR office in any major station, not at the airport machines.

title: Where to Stay in Tokyo: Honest Assessments of Each Neighborhood
excerpt: Every first-time visitor books Shinjuku because someone told them to. Some of those people are right.
heroImageAlt: Aerial view of Tokyo at dusk showing the city lights
author: TABI Editorial

sections.heading: Why neighborhood choice matters
paragraphs: Tokyo has 23 special wards and dozens of distinct neighborhoods, each with a different character and a different argument for why you should sleep there. The decision matters more than most hotel comparison sites suggest. An hour on the Yamanote Line from Ueno to Shibuya is not the same as a ten-minute walk from wherever you're standing. Where you sleep shapes what you do in the morning.

sections.heading: Shinjuku
paragraphs: Shinjuku: the right choice for first-timers who want maximum convenience. Shinjuku Station connects to almost everything. The west side has the skyscraper district and the Keio bus terminal for day trips. The east side has Kabukicho, Golden Gai, and Omoide Yokocho — three different things to do at night. Hotels range from budget capsule options in the ¥3,000–5,000/night range to Park Hyatt at the top. The area is not charming, but it works.

sections.heading: Asakusa
paragraphs: Asakusa: the tourist district, with one of the city's best sightseeing densities within walking distance (Senso-ji, Nakamise, the Sumida River). The downside is that it's quieter outside tourist hours, transport options are limited compared to Shinjuku or Shibuya, and the neighborhood doesn't have the density of restaurants and bars that other areas do. Good for first-timers wanting concentrated sightseeing and a vaguely traditional atmosphere. Less good for eating out late or getting to Shibuya quickly.

sections.heading: Shibuya
paragraphs: Shibuya: better for people on a second or third visit. The neighborhood is in constant construction (it's been under renovation since 2012 and will remain so until approximately 2027). The crossing is iconic and worth seeing once; it's not worth basing a week around. The advantage is easy access to the private rail lines heading west — Shimokitazawa on the Keio Inokashira Line, Nakameguro and Daikanyama within walking distance.

sections.heading: Ginza
paragraphs: Ginza: expensive, convenient, quiet at night. If your priorities are ease of getting around and you're not interested in nightlife, the business hotels here are excellent value relative to the address. Ginza Line goes directly to Shibuya. Walking distance to Tsukiji. Largely tourists and executives, which can feel sterile.

sections.heading: Ueno
paragraphs: Ueno: the most practical choice for travelers with a lot of luggage arriving into Ueno Station, which has direct Shinkansen connections from the north. The area around Ueno Park is dense with museums and cheap restaurants. It has none of Shinjuku's energy, but it has a functioning neighborhood character that Shinjuku lacks — Yanaka and Nezu are walkable from here and are among the most interesting old-city districts in Tokyo.

sections.heading: The general rule
paragraphs: The general rule: within the Yamanote Line loop, you're never more than 20 minutes from anything important. The choice of neighborhood is about what you walk outside to in the morning, not about access. Pick based on the atmosphere you want for breakfast and evening, and trust the train system to handle the rest.

title: Japan's Four Major Ramen Styles: What They Are and Where to Actually Eat Them
excerpt: Ramen in Japan is not one food. Depending on where you are, the word covers a range of things that share almost nothing except noodles.
heroImageAlt: A bowl of Hakata tonkotsu ramen with chashu pork and soft-boiled egg
author: TABI Editorial

sections.heading: Four styles, not one
paragraphs: The four canonical regional ramen styles in Japan are Sapporo (miso), Hakata (tonkotsu), Tokyo (shoyu), and Kitakata (shoyu with flat noodles). This is a simplification. Within each region there are dozens of substyles, proprietary broths, and variations by shop. But the four regional types give you a framework that is more useful than 'ramen is ramen.'

sections.heading: Hakata tonkotsu
paragraphs: Hakata ramen from Fukuoka is the style most likely to become your obsession. The broth is made from pork bones (tonkotsu) simmered for 12 to 18 hours until it turns milky white and rich enough to coat a spoon. The noodles are thin and straight, cooked harder than you'd expect. The correct serving size is small — a half-portion (kogata) is standard, and you're expected to order kaedama (a noodle refill) into the remaining broth. Shops around Hakata Station and the yatai stalls along the Naka River are where to eat it. Budget ¥700–1,000.

sections.heading: Tokyo shoyu
paragraphs: Tokyo shoyu ramen is the gentler style. Chicken or pork-based broth seasoned with soy sauce, usually clearer than Hakata broth, with medium wavy noodles. The toppings are what distinguish one Tokyo shop from another: chashu pork, a soft-boiled egg marinated in soy and mirin, bamboo shoots, nori. The shop Fuunji in Shinjuku makes a tsukemen (dipping ramen) version that requires arriving at 11am or accepting a 45-minute wait.

sections.heading: Sapporo miso
paragraphs: Sapporo miso ramen is the winter version — a heavier broth with miso paste stirred in, often with butter, sweet corn, and bean sprouts on top. It makes sense in Hokkaido in November in a way it doesn't in Tokyo in August. The Susukino neighborhood in Sapporo has the highest concentration of shops worth trying. Expect to spend ¥900–1,200.

sections.heading: How to order
paragraphs: How to order at a ramen shop: most have ticket vending machines at the entrance. Put in your money, select your bowl, take the ticket, and hand it to staff when you sit. If you want a soft-boiled egg (onsen tamago or ajitama), it's usually a separate button. Noodle firmness (kata for firm, yawarakai for soft) and richness (koi for heavy, asa for light) can be specified at some shops. Do not over-specify at your first visit. Order the standard and learn what you're tasting before you start adjusting.

title: How to Watch a Sumo Tournament Without Watching It on TV
