excerpt: Six tournaments a year, three of them in Tokyo. Tickets go on sale months in advance and sell out. Here's how to actually get in.
heroImageAlt: Two sumo wrestlers facing off in the dohyo ring at Ryogoku Kokugikan
author: TABI Editorial

sections.heading: Tournament schedule and cities
paragraphs: Sumo holds six official tournaments (basho) per year, each lasting 15 days. The January, May, and September basho are in Tokyo at Ryogoku Kokugikan. The March basho is in Osaka, July in Nagoya, and November in Fukuoka. If you're planning a trip around attending a tournament, knowing this schedule is step one. January and September in Tokyo are typically the most accessible for international visitors because they don't overlap with Golden Week or summer school holidays.

sections.heading: Getting tickets
paragraphs: Getting tickets: the Japan Sumo Association sells official tickets at Ticket Pia starting roughly two months before the tournament. The best seats (masu-seki, the box seats on the ground floor) sell out in days. What's available to visitors with short notice: elevated arena seats (isu-seki), which start around ¥3,800 for the upper tiers, and same-day door tickets (¥2,200 for the cheap seats) that go on sale at the venue from 8am. The door ticket strategy works — arrive at Ryogoku at 7:30am and you will get in.

sections.heading: What you are watching
paragraphs: What you're watching: sumo matches are short. The actual bout can last anywhere from two seconds to two minutes. The bulk of the time in a sumo tournament is ritual — the ring-entering ceremony (dohyo-iri) for the highest-ranked wrestlers, salt throwing, the slow psychological confrontation before the wrestlers crouch and charge. Understanding that the ceremony is the event, not the prelude to it, changes everything about how to watch.

sections.heading: When to arrive
paragraphs: Timing: the day begins at 8am with the lowest-ranked wrestlers, and the audience trickles in as the day progresses. The top-division matches (makuuchi) begin around 3pm and end around 6pm. Most visitors arrive around 2pm, which is correct. The atmosphere builds through the afternoon. The final few bouts of the day — particularly if a yokozuna or ozeki is involved in a high-stakes match — generate a noise level that is genuinely startling.

sections.heading: Food and drink at Kokugikan
paragraphs: What to eat and drink at the venue: the basement of Kokugikan has chankonabe, the stew traditionally eaten by sumo wrestlers. It is not a gimmick. It's good stew, with chicken and vegetables in a clear dashi broth, and it costs around ¥1,000. There are also yakitori stalls and beer vendors. Food at the masu boxes is served in bento-style sets and can be ordered in advance with tickets.

sections.heading: What surprises you in person
paragraphs: The wrestlers are larger than you expect. The ring is smaller than you expect. The speed of the bouts is faster than you expect. These three surprises reliably improve the live experience over whatever you were imagining from television coverage.

title: The Onsen Rules Nobody Bothers to Tell You
excerpt: The water is shared. The silence is expected. The tattoo policy is more complicated than you've been told.
heroImageAlt: Outdoor rotenburo onsen with steam rising over a stone tub surrounded by bamboo
author: TABI Editorial

sections.heading: The rule everyone needs to know
paragraphs: The fundamental rule of a Japanese onsen or public bath is that you wash before you enter the communal water. This is not optional and not a suggestion. There are shower stations around the perimeter — a stool, a handheld showerhead, shampoo and conditioner in dispensers. You sit on the stool, rinse yourself thoroughly, wash your hair if you're going in with your head back, and then you may enter the bath. Anyone who walks past the shower stations and steps directly into the communal water has misunderstood what they're doing here.

sections.heading: What you bring in
paragraphs: What you bring into the bath: nothing, essentially. A small towel is acceptable if kept out of the water — fold it and put it on your head, or leave it at the edge of the tub. Soap goes at the shower station, not into the bath. Your phone doesn't come into the onsen area at all. This is enforced in most facilities and enforced seriously.

sections.heading: Tattoos: the real policy
paragraphs: Tattoos: the official policy at most traditional onsen is no tattoos, a rule that dates to historical associations between tattoos and organized crime. Enforcement varies significantly. Large resort onsen tend to be stricter; smaller neighborhood sento (public baths) are often more pragmatic. Private family baths (kashikiri buro), bookable by the hour at many ryokan and onsen hotels, have no policy because there are no other guests. If you have visible tattoos and want to use a communal onsen without anxiety, look for facilities with explicit tattoo-friendly policies — they exist and are increasingly common.

sections.heading: Temperature
paragraphs: Temperature: onsen run hot. 40–42°C is standard. 44°C exists. The first immersion will feel excessive. Give yourself 30 seconds to acclimatize before concluding you can't handle it. The correct approach is to enter slowly, sit still, and breathe steadily. You will feel fine within a minute. If you feel dizzy or lightheaded at any point, get out. This is normal and the fault of the heat, not weakness.

sections.heading: Rotating between baths
paragraphs: Rotating baths and timing: most onsen have multiple baths — an indoor main bath, possibly an outdoor rotenburo, a cold plunge bath, a sauna, a carbonated bath. The protocol is to move between them over 30–60 minutes rather than sitting in one until you've cooked. Cold plunge (mizu buro) after a hot soak is optional but converts most people the first time they try it. The contrast is the point.

sections.heading: After the onsen
paragraphs: After the bath: dry off in the changing room before going anywhere else. The post-onsen state — warm, slightly light-headed, skin strangely soft — is best enjoyed horizontal for 15 minutes. Drink water. Many ryokan leave cold water or barley tea in the changing room for this purpose. The evening onsen before bed followed by eight hours of sleep in a cool room is, empirically, the best sleep you will have all year.

title: Don Quijote: What to Buy, What to Skip, and How to Survive the Store
excerpt: Don Quijote is five floors of everything at prices that make no sense. The key is knowing what you came for before you walk in.
heroImageAlt: Don Quijote store exterior at night with colourful signs in Tokyo
author: TABI Editorial

sections.heading: What Don Quijote is
paragraphs: Don Quijote — called Donki by everyone who shops there — is a discount variety store that operates on the principle that if you put enough products in a small enough space at a low enough price, people will buy things they didn't know they needed. This works. The stores are open until 5am. The in-store music is a jingle that plays on loop. The lighting is aggressive. It is not a relaxing shopping experience. It is an effective one.

sections.heading: Skincare and cosmetics
paragraphs: What to buy. Skincare and cosmetics: Donki carries Japanese drugstore brands (Hada Labo, Kose, Shiseido) and some Korean imports at prices below anywhere else you'll find them in Japan. The Hada Labo Gokujyun moisturizing lotion (170ml, around ¥500) is the specific item that beauty content creators have been writing about for years, and the Donki price is genuinely the best in Tokyo. Load up.

sections.heading: Japanese snacks
paragraphs: Japanese snacks: the basement or ground floor of most Donki has a food section with Kit Kat flavors, Pocky, regional chips, and gift-packaged snack sets. If you're buying snacks to take home, this is more convenient than tracking down a specialty snack shop and often cheaper. Green tea Kit Kat in the standard retail package at around ¥300 a box is a reasonable gift that travels well.

sections.heading: Electronics and gadgets
paragraphs: Electronics and gadgets: the electronics section has genuine deals on portable chargers, travel adapters, and USB cables. Don't buy anything complex — laptops and cameras here require Japanese region settings and Japanese warranties. The accessories are fine.

sections.heading: What to skip
paragraphs: What to skip: branded luxury goods in the glass cases near the register. These are legitimate products but the selection is limited and the prices aren't as competitive as the rest of the store. Perfumes are hit or miss. For Japanese whisky, sake, or shochu worth buying, go to a dedicated liquor store.

sections.heading: Navigation
paragraphs: Navigation: the store layout is deliberately non-intuitive. Signage exists but products are stacked floor-to-ceiling in a way that turns navigation into something between a maze and an archaeological dig. Accept this. Give yourself at least 90 minutes for your first visit. Don't try to optimize; let the store find you things. The Shinjuku and Shibuya branches are the largest and most complete. The Akihabara branch is useful if you want the electronics section without the full Donki experience.

title: Hiroshima Is More Than the Peace Park — And the Peace Park Is More Than You Think
excerpt: People visit Hiroshima for one reason. Most leave having found three or four others.
heroImageAlt: The Genbaku Dome atomic bomb memorial in Hiroshima beside the river
author: TABI Editorial

sections.heading: The Peace Memorial Museum
paragraphs: The Peace Memorial Park and Museum is why most people come to Hiroshima, and it should be. The museum documents what happened on August 6, 1945 with a directness and specificity that other memorial museums often avoid — personal objects, shadows burned into stone, recorded testimony. The Children's Peace Monument, the Genbaku Dome, the Flame of Peace that has burned continuously since 1964 and is meant to burn until all nuclear weapons are gone. Budget two to three hours, not one.

sections.heading: Hiroshima beyond the park
paragraphs: What you'll find beyond the memorial. Hiroshima has one of Japan's most interesting covered shotengai (shopping arcade) districts, centered around Hondori and its side streets. It's genuinely local — not a tourist market, but the arcade where people in Hiroshima buy clothes and eat lunch. The okonomiyaki restaurants here are a reason to visit Hiroshima independent of the memorial.

sections.heading: Hiroshima okonomiyaki
paragraphs: Hiroshima okonomiyaki is different from Osaka okonomiyaki. The Osaka version mixes everything together; the Hiroshima version layers ingredients — batter, bean sprouts, cabbage, noodles (yes, noodles), pork, egg — in sequence on a flat griddle. The result is denser and more substantial than the Osaka style. The specific place for it is Okonomimura, a three-floor building near the Hondori arcade with around 25 individual shops each making their version. Pick one that looks busy. Budget ¥1,000–1,400.

sections.heading: Miyajima Island
paragraphs: Miyajima Island: a 10-minute ferry from Hiroshima port (covered by the JR Pass). The vermilion torii gate of Itsukushima Shrine, standing in the sea at high tide, is one of those images that is on every Japan poster and still manages to be more striking in person than in photographs. Walk past the shrine to the hills — Daishoin Temple and the path up to Mt. Misen take you away from the day-trippers within 20 minutes. The island also has deer, which are smaller and less aggressive than Nara's but will absolutely eat your map.

sections.heading: How long to stay
paragraphs: Timing: Hiroshima is a half-day to full-day stop on the standard Tokyo–Kyoto–Osaka route (Shinkansen from Shin-Osaka takes about 85 minutes). A full day allows the museum, Hondori, lunch, and a Miyajima trip. Staying overnight allows a more complete Miyajima experience and access to the city at night, when the riverfront around the Motoyasu-gawa is worth walking.

title: Autumn Leaves in Japan: Where to Go and When the Timing Actually Works
excerpt: Koyo — autumn foliage — is not a fixed event. It starts in Hokkaido in September and ends in Kyushu in December. The timing requires a decision.
heroImageAlt: Maple trees in full autumn colour reflected in a garden pond in Kyoto
author: TABI Editorial

sections.heading: When koyo happens where
paragraphs: Japan's autumn color season (koyo) moves southward and downhill from September through late November. Hokkaido peaks first — late September to mid-October — while central Honshu (Nikko, the Japanese Alps, Kyoto) peaks in early to mid-November. Kyushu and the warmer lowlands finish last, sometimes into December. 'When is the best time for autumn leaves?' depends entirely on where you're going, and the answer shifts by two to four weeks depending on whether you're in the mountains or at sea level.

sections.heading: Kyoto in November
paragraphs: Kyoto's koyo season typically peaks in the second and third weeks of November. The problem: so does Kyoto's tourist density. Tenryu-ji in Arashiyama, Eikan-do in Nanzen-ji, the garden at Tofuku-ji — all of these are spectacular and all of them will be packed. The answer is not to avoid them (the crowds exist because the places are worth it) but to arrange your visit around timing. These gardens look different at 7:30am than at 11am. The light is better and the ratio of leaves to people is significantly higher.

sections.heading: Nikko: the underrated option
paragraphs: The underrated alternative: Nikko, two hours by express from Ueno in Tokyo. The cryptomeria avenue leading to Tosho-gu Shrine, surrounded by beech and maple turning gold and orange, is one of the better koyo experiences in Japan and receives a fraction of Kyoto's November visitors. The Irohazaka switchback road to Lake Chuzenji is the specific spot — worth the drive if you have transport, or a bus if you don't.

sections.heading: The Japanese Alps
paragraphs: The Japanese Alps for serious foliage: Kamikochi (closed after mid-November), Shirakawa-go, and the Tateyama Kurobe Alpine Route offer high-altitude autumn that peaks in October and is genuinely spectacular. Shirakawa-go's thatched-roof farmhouses surrounded by autumn maples is the kind of composition that doesn't require a good photographer — the scene does the work.

sections.heading: Rain and overcast days
paragraphs: What nobody says: rain makes autumn foliage better. The colors are more saturated. The light through wet maple leaves is different from the light through dry ones. An overcast day in Kyoto in November is not a failed day; it's a different photograph and a quieter street. Adjust expectations and pack accordingly.

sections.heading: The practical window
paragraphs: The practical window: if you have flexibility in your travel dates, aim for November 10–20 for central Honshu. Before that, the color is incomplete. After that, the leaves have begun to fall and you're watching the end of something. The narrow peak is what makes koyo worth planning around.

title: Beppu: The Onsen City That's Stranger and Better Than You Expect
excerpt: Beppu produces more geothermal steam per day than anywhere in Japan and possibly anywhere outside Iceland. The city treats this as completely normal.
heroImageAlt: Blue-green geothermal pool steaming at Umi Jigoku in Beppu
author: TABI Editorial

sections.heading: What Beppu is
paragraphs: Beppu is on the northeast coast of Kyushu, about an hour and a half from Fukuoka by limited express train. The city has eight distinct onsen zones (onsen hatto), each with different mineral compositions and water colors — clear, milky white, reddish brown, and the vivid cobalt blue of the Kannawa area. The steam rising off the streets in certain neighborhoods on cool mornings makes it look like the city is slowly cooking from below, which is more or less accurate.

sections.heading: The Hell Tour
paragraphs: The Jigoku Meguri — the Hell Tour — is the tourist attraction: seven pools of geothermally active water that can't be bathed in because they're too hot (98°C in some cases) or too acidic. The pools are genuinely remarkable. Umi Jigoku (Sea Hell) is deep blue and appears to be 200 meters wide. Chinoike Jigoku (Blood Pond Hell) is exactly as red as it sounds. Oniyama Jigoku contains a crocodile enclosure because the groundwater is warm enough to maintain tropical reptiles year-round. A circuit ticket covers all seven for ¥2,200. It's worth it.

sections.heading: Bathing options
paragraphs: The actual bathing options are the reason to stay overnight. Kannawa and the older neighborhood onsens offer public baths (kotoyu) for as little as ¥100–200 — coin-operated, simple tiled rooms, water that does things to your skin that hotel onsen doesn't quite replicate. Hyotan Onsen near Kannawa has eight distinct pools and won a prestigious onsen award; ¥1,300 for full access, open until 1am.

sections.heading: Sand bathing
paragraphs: Sand bathing: Takegawara Onsen in central Beppu offers sunayu — you lie in a shallow pit and staff bury you in geothermally heated sand. The sand temperature is around 50°C, which sounds alarming and is instead comfortable in a way that's difficult to explain until you've experienced it. Fifteen minutes in the sand is roughly equivalent to an hour in a conventional bath in terms of post-session relaxation. ¥1,500, no appointment needed.

sections.heading: Getting there and where to stay
paragraphs: Getting there and where to stay: the Sonic or Sonata limited express from Hakata (Fukuoka) takes 1h45m–2h and costs around ¥4,500 (or covered by JR Pass). The city is small enough that the main onsen areas are walkable from the station or a ¥700 taxi ride. Ryokan here are genuinely affordable compared to Hakone — ¥10,000–15,000 per person including dinner and breakfast is achievable. Beppu makes a strong case for being the most accessible serious onsen town in Japan.

title: Japanese Stationery Is Worth the Suitcase Space
excerpt: Pens, notebooks, tape, paper clips. Japan has turned the mundane into an argument for why other countries aren't trying hard enough.
heroImageAlt: Rows of colourful MT washi tape rolls and stationery at a Japanese stationery shop
author: TABI Editorial

sections.heading: The Pilot Hi-Tec-C case for Japanese stationery
paragraphs: The Pilot Hi-Tec-C is a 0.3mm gel pen that costs ¥220 at any stationery shop in Japan and writes with a precision that no comparable pen in Europe or North America has matched at any price. This is not enthusiasm. It is a factual statement about the difference between the Japanese stationery industry and everyone else's, and it is the reason you should set aside half a day for stationery shopping.

sections.heading: The main chains
paragraphs: The two main chains: Itoya in Ginza (12 floors, a serious institutional stationery store that's been operating since 1904) and Loft (department store format, wide selection, multiple Tokyo locations). Itoya is for people who want to be deliberate about what they buy and spend two hours doing it. Loft is for people who want variety and efficiency. Tokyu Hands, now rebranded as Hands, has the broadest range of craft and stationery supplies.

sections.heading: Hobonichi planners and Midori notebooks
paragraphs: What to buy. Hobonichi planners: a cult Japanese planner published by the company that made Mother 2. Available in several formats (Techo, Weeks, Cousin); the standard Techo is an A6 daily planner printed on Tomoe River paper, which is 52gsm and shows through ink without bleeding. ¥3,300 at official stores. Midori MD paper products: notebooks and pads using MD paper, which accepts fountain pen ink unusually well. The A5 blank notebook is ¥900.

sections.heading: MT masking tape
paragraphs: MT masking tape: washi tape in a range of patterns and widths, sold individually at ¥200–400 a roll. The flagship store in Ginza (MT Lab) has several hundred patterns. The tape is removable, repositionable, and takes ink without smearing — it has acquired a following among journalers and planners globally that has made the brand an export success, though the selection in Japan is larger and cheaper than anywhere else.

sections.heading: Pens worth buying
paragraphs: For pens: the Uni-ball One (gel ink, available in 0.38mm and 0.5mm, multiple colors, ¥300) and the Zebra Mildliner (a dual-tip highlighter with pastel tones that don't cause eye fatigue) are the two most commonly recommended items by people who work with paper professionally. Both are widely available and inexpensive enough to buy in quantity.

sections.heading: The suitcase argument
paragraphs: Suitcase space: stationery is light and flat. A full day's shopping fits in a document bag. The Pilot Hi-Tec-C pens are ¥220 each and weigh nothing. The Hobonichi is the same size as a passport. This is one of the gift categories where there's no excuse not to buy for everyone you know.

title: Eating at a Sushi Counter: What to Expect and How to Not Ruin It
excerpt: The sushi counter is not intimidating once you understand that there are three rules, they're easy, and the chef wants you to enjoy it.
heroImageAlt: A sushi chef preparing nigiri behind a counter with fresh fish displayed in a glass case
author: TABI Editorial

sections.heading: Counter sushi vs conveyor belt
paragraphs: Counter sushi (omakase or a la carte) is different from kaiten (conveyor belt) in format, price, and the nature of the relationship between you and the chef. At the counter, there are usually 8–12 seats and a clear glass case of today's fish. You either order a set omakase (chef's choice, typically ¥5,000–20,000 per person) or point at items in the case and they're made in front of you. The fish was at the market this morning. The rice is slightly warm. The whole operation is built around immediacy.

sections.heading: Three rules
paragraphs: Three rules. One: eat the sushi as soon as it's placed in front of you. The rice is seasoned and tempered for eating now, not in three minutes. Sushi that sits at room temperature changes. Two: it is acceptable to eat nigiri with your fingers. This is actually the traditional method. Using chopsticks risks the topping sliding off and is optional. Three: dip fish-side down into soy sauce, not rice-side down (soy sauce in the rice makes it fall apart and oversalts the bite).

sections.heading: What to order
paragraphs: What to order if you can. Tuna in its hierarchy: chutoro (medium fatty tuna) over maguro (lean tuna) over otoro (full fatty tuna) — not because otoro isn't extraordinary, but because it's overwhelming in large quantities. The best first piece at a sushi counter is usually whatever the chef puts in front of you first, which is often a lighter white fish like hirame (flounder) or suzuki (sea bass) designed to calibrate your palate.

sections.heading: Ginger and wasabi
paragraphs: Ginger (gari) is a palate cleanser, eaten between pieces, not on top of them. Wasabi is incorporated into the sushi by the chef at a traditional counter — additional wasabi on the side is available but signals that you found the amount insufficient. This is fine. Ask.

sections.heading: Budget guidance
paragraphs: Budget guidance: the most common misconception about counter sushi in Japan is that it's prohibitively expensive. At a mid-range counter in Tokyo away from Ginza, ¥3,000–5,000 buys a satisfying a la carte meal of 8–10 pieces plus tea. The ¥20,000+ experience exists and is extraordinary, but it's not the entry point. Lunch sets at sushi restaurants often offer the same fish as dinner at 30–40% lower prices. Tsukiji Outer Market, Ueno, and the side streets of Shinjuku have the highest density of good-value options.

title: Nara Beyond the Deer: What to Do After the First Hour
excerpt: Yes, the deer are free-roaming and yes, they will bow to get crackers from you. This takes about 40 minutes. Nara has a full day's worth of things after that.
heroImageAlt: Sika deer roaming free in Nara Park with Todai-ji temple visible through the trees
author: TABI Editorial

sections.heading: The deer
paragraphs: Nara Park has approximately 1,200 sika deer that have been designated national treasures and have, over generations of contact with tourists, learned that a particular bowing gesture triggers cracker-giving. You can buy shika-senbei (deer crackers) from vendors at ¥200 a pack. The deer will absolutely approach you. Some will grab your bag. One will probably bite you lightly on the jacket. This is fine and expected and takes under an hour before it becomes routine. The question is what you do with the rest of the day.

sections.heading: Todai-ji
paragraphs: Todai-ji Temple: the largest wooden building in the world and home to a 15-meter bronze Buddha (the Nara Daibutsu) that has been sitting in approximately the same position since 752 AD. Entrance is ¥600. The scale is genuinely difficult to convey — the Buddha's nostril is the size of a small door, and there is a pillar inside the building with a hole at the base that's the same diameter as the nostril, which children (and optimistic adults) attempt to crawl through as a matter of ritual. Budget 45 minutes.

sections.heading: Kasuga Taisha
paragraphs: Kasuga Taisha: a Shinto shrine established in 768 AD, famous for its 3,000 stone and bronze lanterns that are lit twice a year (Setsubun in February and Obon in August). The path through the cedar forest to reach it is as good as the shrine itself — moss-covered stone lanterns lining the approach, deer wandering between the cedars. Entry to the outer grounds is free; the inner precinct is ¥500.

sections.heading: Naramachi
paragraphs: Naramachi: the preserved merchant town directly south of Nara Park. Narrow alleys, machiya townhouses, textile shops, sake breweries, and craft cafés — the kind of neighborhood that was here 200 years ago and has been left largely intact. It's quieter than the park area and full of places to eat. Nakatanidou mochi shop has a famous kinako mochi (pounded rice cake) that queues extend to the street; the Kasugayama area has older restaurants with proper set lunch menus.

sections.heading: Getting there and timing
paragraphs: Nara is 45 minutes from Kyoto by JR (¥730 or JR Pass) or about the same on the Kintetsu Line. It works as a half-day or full-day trip from either Kyoto or Osaka. The main park and Todai-ji can be crowded in the morning; the deer and crowds thin significantly after 3pm. If you're coming on a weekday and can arrive at 8:30am, the park before the day-trippers show up is a genuinely different experience.

title: Golden Week: What It Is, Why It Will Destroy Your Trip, and When to Go Instead
excerpt: Golden Week runs from late April to early May. If your Japan trip overlaps with it, you have two options: embrace the chaos or reschedule.
heroImageAlt: Crowded hanami cherry blossom viewing in Ueno Park during Golden Week
author: TABI Editorial

sections.heading: What Golden Week is
paragraphs: Golden Week is Japan's largest consecutive holiday period — a cluster of four national holidays between April 29 and May 5 that most companies treat as a full week off. Around 20 million Japanese people travel domestically during this window. International visitors add to this. The result is that every popular destination, every Shinkansen, every tourist attraction, and every hotel in Japan is at or near maximum capacity simultaneously.

sections.heading: What actually happens
paragraphs: What actually happens: the Tokaido Shinkansen from Tokyo to Osaka sells out weeks in advance and runs extra services that also sell out. Popular ryokan are fully booked four to six months ahead. The queue for Fushimi Inari in Kyoto, normally manageable at 7am, is significant by 8am. Hakone accommodation triples in price. Narita arrivals and departures are maximally busy. None of this is impossible to navigate; it's just not the Japan trip most people imagine.

sections.heading: If you are already booked
paragraphs: If you're already booked during Golden Week: lean into it. Go to places that are interesting specifically when full of Japanese people rather than tourists. City parks during Golden Week — Yoyogi in Tokyo, Maruyama in Kyoto — are excellent: families with picnic setups, food stalls, outdoor performances, a festive atmosphere that you don't get in October. The experience is genuinely different rather than categorically worse.

sections.heading: Shinkansen strategy
paragraphs: The Shinkansen strategy: if you have a JR Pass, reserved seats on Shinkansen are essential. Reserve them the moment you activate your pass. Non-reserved cars during Golden Week are standing-room only from Tokyo to Kyoto. If you don't have a JR Pass, buy Shinkansen tickets as far in advance as possible through Ekinet or at any JR ticket office.

sections.heading: When to go instead
paragraphs: When to go instead: late October and early November for autumn color with reasonable crowds. January for minimum tourists and maximum winter onsen value. Late September, after the summer humidity breaks. The weeks immediately after Golden Week ends (May 10–25) are excellent — the weather is mild, crowds have dispersed, and hotel rates drop back to normal.

title: What to Buy When You Buy Japanese Sake
excerpt: Sake is rice wine in the same way that champagne is grape juice. The category is more complex than most people expect, and the range of quality is enormous.
heroImageAlt: Rows of sake bottles at a specialist sake shop in Japan
author: TABI Editorial

sections.heading: The two main variables
paragraphs: Sake is fermented rice, water, yeast, and koji mold. The main variables: how much the rice has been polished before brewing (the seimaibuai, expressed as a percentage of the grain remaining), and whether distilled alcohol has been added. These two factors create the main classification categories that appear on labels and that are worth understanding before you buy anything.

sections.heading: Classifications that matter
paragraphs: The classifications that matter. Junmai: pure rice sake, no added alcohol. Honjozo: a small amount of distilled alcohol added, which lightens the sake and adds aroma. Ginjo: rice polished to at least 60% (40% milled away), more aromatic. Daiginjo: polished to at least 50%, the top tier, typically the most delicate and expensive. Combine these: Junmai Daiginjo is pure rice, top-tier polish, usually the premium product. Futsushu is table sake — no premium designation, reasonable for everyday drinking.

sections.heading: What to actually buy
paragraphs: What to actually buy. For a gift or something to drink on the trip: a Junmai Ginjo or Junmai Daiginjo from a mid-sized regional brewery priced between ¥2,000 and ¥5,000 (720ml). The big names (Dassai, Kubota, Hakkaisan) are reliably good and widely available in department stores and specialist shops; they became famous because they're excellent. Dassai 23 (polished to 23% remaining) is exceptional and expensive (around ¥5,000 for 300ml); Dassai 39 is similar in character at two-thirds the price.

sections.heading: Nigori sake
paragraphs: Nigori sake (unfiltered, cloudy, with rice sediment) is best drunk cold and fresh — it doesn't travel as well. If you're drinking in Japan, try it; if you're buying to bring home, stick to clear filtered sake in sealed bottles.

sections.heading: Where to buy
paragraphs: Where to buy: department store basement floors (depachika) have the best curated range and staff who can explain what they're selling. Specialist sake shops (sakaya) exist in most cities — Tokyo's Hasegawa Saketen chain is a reliable option with English-speaking staff at some locations. Avoid convenience store sake for gifts; it's functional but unmemorable.

sections.heading: Storage and shelf life
paragraphs: Storage and shelf life: premium sake is best kept refrigerated and consumed within a few months of purchase. If you're transporting it home, keep it cold where possible and drink it within six months. Nigori in particular degrades faster. Honjozo and Futsushu are more forgiving. Buy what you can drink.

title: Hokkaido in July: Japan's Best-Kept Summer Secret
excerpt: Tokyo in July is 35 degrees and 90% humidity. Hokkaido in July is 22 degrees and clear. These two facts explain a lot.
heroImageAlt: Lavender fields in full bloom at Farm Tomita in Furano, Hokkaido
author: TABI Editorial

sections.heading: The climate case
paragraphs: Hokkaido is Japan's northernmost island and its least densely populated. The summer climate is a direct consequence of this geography: while central Honshu is enduring a summer that most temperate-climate visitors find difficult, Hokkaido is experiencing something close to a Northern European July. Average temperatures in Sapporo in July run 22–25°C, with lower humidity. It's possible to walk outside without immediately sweating through your clothes. For visitors arriving from humid climates, this discovery is approximately life-changing.

sections.heading: Lavender fields in Furano
paragraphs: The lavender fields of Furano are the most photographed image of Hokkaido summer: long rows of purple lavender against a backdrop of hills and clouds, peaking in early to mid-July. Farm Tomita in Furano is the most famous, and is legitimately beautiful despite being a tourist operation. Come early morning. The crowds arrive by 10am. The lavender at 7am with mist still on the hills is the real version of the photograph you've seen.

sections.heading: Biei and the Panorama Road
paragraphs: Biei: a small town 20 kilometers north of Furano with rolling farmland — patchwork fields of potato, wheat, sunflower, and canola — that attracts photographers specifically for the quality of the light in summer. Renting a bicycle and spending the morning on the Panorama Road is the correct approach. No specific sight to reach; the road and the fields are the destination.

sections.heading: Sapporo
paragraphs: Sapporo: the capital and the city that functions as the base. The Odori Park beer garden runs all summer (July–August) — rows of tables under the elm trees with brewery stalls from Sapporo, Asahi, Kirin, and Suntory running simultaneously. This is worth a single evening on its own terms. Soup curry, a Sapporo invention, is served in most restaurants here and is one of those regional dishes that makes no sense until you've eaten it.

sections.heading: Daisetsuzan National Park
paragraphs: The Daisetsuzan National Park: the largest national park in Japan, an alpine region accessible from multiple trailheads. The ropeway at Asahidake gives access to volcanic terrain above 1,500 meters where the hiking is excellent and the views extend across the island. A serious day-hike into the high terrain will not intersect with many other people and looks nothing like the rest of Japan.

sections.heading: Getting there
paragraphs: Getting there: Sapporo (New Chitose Airport) is 1h40m from Tokyo by air. Domestic flights are cheap — book at least three weeks in advance for ¥8,000–15,000 return. Once you're there, a rental car is the right choice for Furano and Biei; trains exist but the timing is inconvenient for covering multiple areas in a day.

title: Koenji and Shimokitazawa: Tokyo for People Who've Already Done Harajuku
excerpt: Two stops on different lines. Two neighborhoods that run on thrift stores, live music, and a specific refusal to be improved.
heroImageAlt: Narrow street in Shimokitazawa lined with vintage clothing shops and cafes
author: TABI Editorial

sections.heading: What these neighborhoods are
paragraphs: Harajuku is famous. Shimokitazawa is known. Koenji is where people who've lived in Tokyo for five years go when they want to be reminded what the city felt like before everything got expensive and curated. Both Koenji and Shimokitazawa deserve an afternoon each, and the fastest way to understand why is to walk from the station in either direction without a destination.

sections.heading: Koenji: the vintage ecosystem
paragraphs: Koenji, on the Chuo Line, is Tokyo's vintage district — not vintage-as-aesthetic but vintage-as-ecosystem. The streets around the north and south exits of the station contain somewhere between 50 and 80 second-hand clothing shops, each specializing in something different: one does only 70s Western denim, another specializes in military surplus, a third has nothing but vintage sportswear priced by the kilo. The Awa Odori festival in August draws around 300,000 people to this neighborhood specifically, making it briefly the loudest and most populated neighborhood in all of Tokyo.

sections.heading: Koenji: bars and izakaya
paragraphs: The coffee shops and bars in Koenji are a separate argument for visiting. Pure, a jazz bar in continuous operation since the 1980s, plays vinyl records on equipment that hasn't changed since opening. There are izakaya here that predate the bubble economy and have no interest in attracting anyone who found them on an app. The food is good and cheap: a tonkatsu set lunch is ¥950, a yakitori skewer is ¥120.

sections.heading: Shimokitazawa: live music
paragraphs: Shimokitazawa, accessible via the Odakyu Line from Shinjuku or Keio Inokashira from Shibuya, has a different character. It's the live music neighborhood: more than 30 small music venues (livehouse) are within a 10-minute walk of the station, ranging from 50-person basement rooms to 500-capacity halls. Any night of the week, most of them have shows. Tickets are typically ¥2,000–3,000 at the door.

sections.heading: The Sunday market
paragraphs: The Sunday market at Shimokitazawa draws vintage sellers, plant vendors, record collectors, and food stalls to the streets outside the station. It's a neighborhood event rather than a tourist attraction, and has the energy of something that's been happening every week for years and has no interest in being photographed for social media. Show up around 11am with no plan and spend two hours. That's the correct approach to Shimokitazawa.

title: How to Actually Eat Wagyu Beef in Japan
excerpt: Wagyu is expensive. This is not a misunderstanding. The question is what you're getting for the expense, and whether a ¥5,000 lunch version is the same as a ¥30,000 dinner.
heroImageAlt: Thinly sliced wagyu beef with visible marbling on a wooden board
author: TABI Editorial

sections.heading: What wagyu actually is
paragraphs: Wagyu refers to four specific Japanese beef cattle breeds — Black, Brown, Shorthorn, and Polled — of which Japanese Black (Kuroge Wagashu) is the one you'll encounter at restaurants. The defining characteristic is intramuscular fat (shimofuri), distributed through the muscle in a marbling pattern that creates the butter-soft texture wagyu is known for. The marbling is graded A1 to A5 by the Japan Meat Grading Association; A5 is the maximum.

sections.heading: Kobe, Matsusaka, and Omi
paragraphs: The three regions you'll encounter most often. Kobe beef (from Tajima-strain cattle in Hyogo Prefecture): the most famous internationally, and genuinely excellent, though supply is far smaller than the global market for 'Kobe beef' implies — most 'Kobe beef' outside Japan is not from Kobe. Matsusaka beef from Mie Prefecture is considered by many Japanese food professionals to be superior in fat quality; it's less famous internationally because nobody started a marketing campaign for it in the 1990s. Omi beef from Shiga, one of the oldest beef brands in Japan, is the option for people who've read enough about wagyu to want the historical footnote.

sections.heading: Formats and prices
paragraphs: Formats and pricing. A5 wagyu as yakiniku (grilled at the table over charcoal) is the most common restaurant format — ¥3,000–8,000 for a plate of premium cuts, best shared. A5 wagyu sukiyaki (simmered in a sweet soy broth with tofu and vegetables) is the winter version and is slightly more forgiving for first-timers because the broth moderates the richness. Wagyu as a steak, Western-style, is available in department store restaurants and high-end steakhouses — expect ¥15,000–40,000 per person for the premium experience.

sections.heading: The accessible option
paragraphs: The practical option: department store basement food halls (depachika) in Tokyo, Osaka, and Kyoto sell wagyu by the slice for home cooking and often have prepared wagyu bentos and small yakiniku sets. A high-quality wagyu beef bowl (gyudon) at a specialist restaurant like Yoshida Gyu in Tokyo can be had for ¥1,500–3,000 and gives a genuine sense of what A4–A5 wagyu tastes like without the full restaurant-dinner commitment.

sections.heading: The one thing worth knowing
paragraphs: The one thing worth knowing: wagyu at A5 is rich. A small amount satisfies in a way that lean beef doesn't. At a proper yakiniku restaurant, a 100g portion is enough for one person. The instinct to order more than you can eat is strong and almost always incorrect. Order one plate, taste it properly, and then decide if you want another.

title: Japan's Vending Machines Are a Serious Subject
excerpt: There are 4 million vending machines in Japan. One for every 31 people. This is not an accident.
heroImageAlt: A row of colourful Japanese vending machines on a city street at night
author: TABI Editorial

sections.heading: The density
paragraphs: Japan has more vending machines per capita than any country except possibly Singapore. They appear outside train stations, on mountain trails, inside office buildings, at the end of rural roads where nothing else exists for kilometers. The density is a logistics achievement: the machines are serviced on fixed schedules, calibrated to local demand, and stocked with whatever the neighborhood apparently wants at the hour it apparently wants it.

sections.heading: What is for sale
paragraphs: What's for sale: the most common machines stock canned and bottled drinks — hot canned coffee, cold green tea, sports drinks, canned beer, occasionally alcohol. The hot/cold switching happens seasonally and sometimes twice daily — a button will show HOT or COLD depending on which side of the internal temperature shelf the product is resting on. Buying a hot Boss Coffee from a vending machine in February is one of those simple pleasures that people consistently describe as an unexpectedly significant memory from Japan.

sections.heading: Specialty machines
paragraphs: The specialty machines: some train stations have instant ramen machines with a surprisingly wide variety. Umbrella machines appear outside train stations when rain is forecast and charge ¥500–800 for a basic transparent umbrella. There are machines selling fresh bananas, hot pizza by the slice, live stag beetles in summer (this is real), and surgical masks in quantities ranging from one to fifty.

sections.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Pricing: drinks at vending machines are ¥130–200 for most items, approximately the same as a convenience store. The premium is zero or marginal. Alcohol (beer, chuhai) is available late-night where the machine is located outside a convenience store or near a station — age verification is on the honor system at the machine level, though some have a button for adult confirmation.

sections.heading: IC card payment
paragraphs: IC card payment: most modern vending machines accept Suica or ICOCA. Tap the card reader, the drink drops, and the amount is deducted. It's faster than coins, which in Japan are otherwise still the default payment method for vending. The transition to contactless is quicker here than at cash registers, perhaps because the machine doesn't need to be retrained.

sections.heading: The cultural point
paragraphs: The cultural point: vending machines in Japan are clean, they work (machine failure is rare enough to be noteworthy), and they stock things people actually want. This seems basic until you've been somewhere where vending machines are a coin trap of expired merchandise and broken change returns. Japan's machines are infrastructure, and like most Japanese infrastructure, they are taken seriously.

title: Day Trips From Tokyo That Aren't Hakone or Nikko
excerpt: Hakone is excellent. Nikko is impressive. Both are in every guidebook and overrun on weekends. There are better options if you're willing to look slightly further.
heroImageAlt: Kurazukuri Street in Kawagoe with traditional clay-walled Edo-period storehouses
author: TABI Editorial

sections.heading: The problem with the usual recommendations
paragraphs: The standard day-trip recommendations from Tokyo are Hakone (hot springs, Fuji views), Nikko (elaborate Edo-period shrines in the mountains), and Kamakura (giant Buddha, seaside). All three are worth visiting. All three are also predictable, and in peak season they're handling tourist numbers that test their infrastructure. If you've already been to any of them, or if you're planning a second Tokyo visit and want to branch out, there are options that deliver comparable experiences with significantly smaller crowds.

sections.heading: Kawagoe
paragraphs: Kawagoe, 30 minutes from Ikebukuro on the Tobu Tojo Line (around ¥470). Called Little Edo, it has a preserved merchant district (Kurazukuri Street) with Edo-period clay-walled warehouses that have become antique shops, sweet shops, and cafés. The bell tower that rings every six hours has been doing so since 1624. The Kashiya Yokocho (Candy Lane) alley sells old-fashioned Japanese sweets from small family shops. Significantly fewer international visitors than Kamakura; similar historical atmosphere.

sections.heading: Chichibu
paragraphs: Chichibu, 90 minutes from Ikebukuro on the Seibu Chichibu Line. A mountain town with a famous Shinto shrine (Chichibu Shrine), a series of 34 Buddhist pilgrimage temples that serious walkers circuit over multiple days, and the only natural karst formation in the Kanto region at Ootaki. In December, the Chichibu Night Festival (December 2–3) is one of Japan's largest float festivals and draws 200,000 people — still far more manageable than any equivalent Tokyo-area event.

sections.heading: Izu Peninsula
paragraphs: Izu Peninsula, 90 minutes from Tokyo by Shinkansen to Atami or Mishima. A volcanic peninsula with hot springs, dramatic coastline, and fewer tourists per kilometer than anywhere in the Hakone area. Shuzenji in the central valley is a small onsen town with a classic feel — a bamboo grove along the river, wooden onsen hotels, a 9th-century Buddhist temple. Enough for a full day without a crowd problem.

sections.heading: Mashiko
paragraphs: Mashiko, two hours from Ueno by train (JR Utsunomiya plus bus). A ceramics town that became famous when the potter Shoji Hamada established a studio there in the 1920s and invited other ceramicists. There are now around 300 kilns in and around the town. The spring and autumn pottery fairs are among the best craft markets in Japan. The Hamada Shoji Memorial Mashiko Sankokan museum is on the original estate and worth two hours. The journey requires planning but the experience is unique.

title: Omiyage: The Art of Bringing Back the Right Thing
excerpt: Returning from anywhere in Japan without gifts for the people you left behind is not a neutral act. Omiyage culture runs deep, and buying well requires knowing the rules.
heroImageAlt: Beautifully packaged Japanese confectionery boxes displayed at a station gift shop
author: TABI Editorial

sections.heading: What omiyage is
paragraphs: Omiyage — travel souvenirs intended for others — is a structured practice in Japan, not an afterthought. When Japanese people return from a trip, they bring back regional food gifts for coworkers, family, and neighbors. The obligation is real enough that Japanese airports, train stations, and tourist areas are built around it: the gift shops at major stations are not concessions but primary retail destinations designed for the specific purpose of providing appropriately packaged regional food at prices calibrated for group gifting.

sections.heading: What makes good omiyage
paragraphs: What constitutes good omiyage. The best gifts are things that are genuinely regional — a sweet or confection from a specific prefecture that can't be bought in Tokyo or wherever you're returning to. Kyoto's Yatsuhashi (cinnamon-flavored triangular confections, raw or baked, in a range of fillings), Hiroshima's Momiji Manju (maple-leaf-shaped cakes with red bean or cream filling), Sapporo's Royce chocolate — these are the canonical examples, and they work because they represent a place specifically. Generic Japan gifts (things with sakura or Mt. Fuji printed on them) are less appreciated.

sections.heading: Format matters
paragraphs: Format matters. Omiyage is given in individually portioned packages, usually 6, 10, or 15 pieces, because it will be shared across a group. A single item that can't be divided is not omiyage. The packaging is typically beautiful and part of the gift — Japanese confectionery packaging has been developed over centuries into a minor art form.

sections.heading: What to bring home
paragraphs: For the visitor bringing things home to other countries: apply the same logic. Regional Japanese sweets in their original packaging travel well, communicate where you've been specifically, and arrive looking intentional. Matcha Kit Kats and regional chip flavors are the casual version. For something more considered: Hikari Kinako (roasted soybean flour snacks from Kyoto), wagashi (traditional confections) from an established Kyoto confectioner like Toraya, or a small tin of gyokuro tea from Uji.

sections.heading: Where to buy
paragraphs: Where to buy: the station gift shops (ekimachibu) in major Shinkansen stations have the best regional selection for your exact route. Kyoto Station has an excellent floor of Kyoto-specific gifts. Tokyo Station's basement has confections from across Japan, which is useful if you forgot to buy in-region but want regional gifts. Department stores have the most curated selection at the highest price; convenience store gift sections exist but should be a last resort.

title: The Hidden Shrines of Kyoto Locals Still Keep Secret
summary: Beyond Fushimi Inari and Kinkaku-ji lies another Kyoto — quiet, moss-covered, and entirely yours. We asked local monks and longtime residents where they actually go when the crowds become too much. These are the shrines that rarely appear in guidebooks.

title: How to Spend 10 Days in Japan Without a Single Tourist Trap
summary: Most first-time visitors follow the same Golden Route — Tokyo, Kyoto, Osaka — and come home feeling like they saw a theme park. This itinerary takes a different approach: slower, deeper, and built around the neighborhoods that locals actually use.

title: The Perfect Route up Mt. Fuji: What Nobody Tells You
summary: Climbing Fuji is one of those experiences that sounds straightforward until you're doing it at 3am in a headlamp queue. The mountain rewards preparation and punishes assumptions — here's what we learned from five ascents across different seasons.

title: Osaka in 3 Days: The Eating and Wandering Itinerary
summary: Osaka has a reputation as Japan's kitchen, but that barely scratches the surface. This city rewards those who eat their way through it without a plan — we've structured the chaos into three days that still feel spontaneous.

title: Yakushima: Japan's Ancient Forest That Few Tourists Find
summary: A UNESCO-listed island covered in cedar trees that are older than many civilizations, Yakushima sits off the southern tip of Kyushu and sees a fraction of the visitors it deserves. The hike through Shiratani Unsuikyo inspired Princess Mononoke — and it still feels that way.

title: How to Read a Japanese Menu Without Speaking Japanese
summary: Most Japanese restaurants don't have English menus, and that's not a problem once you know what to look for. A handful of kanji, some pattern recognition, and the willingness to point gets you through almost any restaurant in the country.

title: What Wabi-Sabi Really Means — And Why the World Needs It Now
summary: The Japanese aesthetic of imperfection and transience offers something Western design philosophy rarely does: permission to be incomplete. Wabi-sabi isn't a style — it's a way of perceiving, and learning to see it changes how you look at almost everything.

title: Kintsugi: The Art of Repairing Broken Things with Gold
summary: A 500-year-old technique for mending ceramics with lacquer mixed with gold, silver, or platinum has become a global metaphor for resilience. We visited a master craftsman in Kyoto to understand why kintsugi is about far more than broken bowls.

title: Hanami 2026: Where to See Cherry Blossoms Like a Local
summary: Skip the tourist crowds at Maruyama Park and Ueno. The best hanami spots are the ones you stumble into — a neighborhood embankment, a school gate, a canal nobody bothered to list. We've found them, so you don't have to.

title: The Best Japanese Kitchen Knives to Bring Home
summary: Japanese knives are the single best souvenir you can carry out of Japan — functional, beautiful, and something you'll use every day for decades. This guide covers every price point from a ¥4,000 entry knife to the hand-forged blade worth saving up for.

title: Buying Matcha: Ceremonial vs. Culinary Grade Explained
summary: The matcha market is flooded with products that taste nothing like what you drink in a tea ceremony — and the price tag is not a reliable guide. Here's how to read labels, what to taste for, and where to buy the real thing.

title: 10 Japanese Drugstore Products Worth Every Yen
summary: Japanese pharmacies are overwhelming in the best way — walls of skincare, hair treatments, and stomach remedies at prices that make no sense until you try them. These are the ten products that actually earn their place in your luggage.

title: Where to Buy Authentic Yukata in Tokyo Without the Markup
summary: The tourist-district yukata sold near Asakusa are fine, but the ones sold at department store basements and specialist shops are extraordinary — and often cheaper. This guide takes you past the obvious options to the places worth visiting.
